“permit of any necessary inferences from its contingence to God” (Torrance, 1969, p. 62). God did 
not have to make the world this way, but chose to graciously create a world that humans could 
interact with, and in some way, come to understand through exploration. Like the parent who gets 
great pleasure from watching their children romp around a forest or new playground and examine 
some details of their environment, God gets great delight in watching his children explore what he 
has created on his vast playground.7

Consequently, while the created realm is knowable, the physical world can only speak about 
God in ambiguous or vague terms (Torrance, 1982). For Torrance (1971), theology is the unique 
science that is devoted to knowing God, while the natural sciences and social sciences are the “rig- 
orous and disciplined extension” of that basic way of knowing through nature that is applied to 
“every area of human life and thought” (p. 91). Moreover, the psychologist who discovers aspects 
of these orders of creation or the human creature may or may not ultimately be drawn back to a 
belief in God. Even if a scientist or psychologist might be drawn to a belief in God from studying 
the order of creation, the full revelation of who God is in Jesus Christ is not possible without the 
working of special revelation. Or said another way, while general revelation through creation points 
back to God as the Creator, it does not necessarily point back to God as the redeemer of creation.

7.  “God leads a very interesting life and is full of joy,” Dallas Willard remarks: “Undoubtedly he is the most 
joyous being in the universe ... We pay a lot of money to get a tank with a few tropical fish in it ... but 
God has seas full of them which he constantly enjoys” (Willard, 1998, pp. 62-63).

 52

Journal of Psychology and Theology 48( I )

Fourth, and a foundational element for those practicing integration, is the acknowledgement that 
creation and the human creature possess an order that is discernable through the psychological sei- 
enees. How much truth that one believes can be discerned through psychology determines the vari- 
ous positions within the integration dialogue. As mentioned earlier, the dictum that is often used in 
integration literature is “truth is one” or “all truth is God’s truth.” It is important to note that state- 
ments like “all truth is God’s truth” assume an understanding of revelation that God’s truths are 
revealed in creation, that these truths are knowable, and that truth can be discovered in the wider 
world. While these elements are widely regarded among those practicing integration, what is not 
widely acknowledged is how integrationists will differ as to how much of the world (or the human 
person) is discernable to humans through the study of psychology depending on their theological 
assumptions regarding the doctrine of creation, revelation, and how sin affects human apprehension 
of truth.8 If the noetic effects of sin remain the focus in the integration discussion without acknowl- 
edging the doctrine of creation, then the dialogue tends to center on the corruption of psychology or 
the sciences. However, balancing the doctrine of creation with the noetic effects of sin enables one 
to speak to aspects of truth within the world and within the creature, while also acknowledging that 
these truths that are discovered do not necessarily shed light on humanity’s estranged relationship 
from God and redemption offered in Jesus Christ (Barth, 1963). Calvin acknowledges that the noetic 
effects of sin and the fall do cormpt human reason, but even with this corruption, the order of crea- 
tion still allows for knowledge of earthly things like government, managing a household, mechani- 
cal skills, and the liberal arts (Calvin, 1960). The corruption that Calvin primarily discusses remains 
humanity’s inability to grasp spiritual wisdom of “heavenly things” (Calvin, 1960). Sin does blind 
humans to see the truth of Christ, but this blinding is not equal in all areas (Bloesch, 1997). Sin is 
pervasive in human life, but to infer that all goodness in humanity is lost, or that humanity has no 
capacity or ability to reason through disciplines like psychology, is to ultimately elevate the doctrine 
of sin above the doctrines of creation and redemption (Gunton, 2002). An elevation of the doctrine 
of sin over the doctrine of creation is commonplace among evangelical practitioners within the the- 
ology of integration, and the proper balancing of creation, along with sin and revelation, is an impor- 
tant step in order to understand how truth can be gleaned through psychology. Theological 
anthropology from a Christian perspective explores the intricacies of human personhood as it is 
rooted in the triune God, while psychology (from a Christian theological perspective) is the rigorous 
study of the order and nature of the human being while acknowledging the human person is a created 
reality that God has made (Gunton, 2002; Shults, 2003).

8. For example, the helpful and popular integrative work, Psychology and Christianity: Five views (edited 

by Eric Johnson), proposes five different viewpoints on how Christian faith integration should be done. 
Each viewpoint provides helpful features that demonstrate the variety of ways that faith integration can 
take place within psychology. Looking at the work through the lens of the doctrine of creation and rev- 
elation, the spectrum of the “five” views exists primarily because the authors hold differing theological 
assumptions regarding creation and general and special revelation, though this is not explicitly stated. 
More specifically the various authors’ views (generally speaking) line up along a spectrum to the degree 
that they believe the order of creation (truth) is discernable through human investigation of the world 
through psychological study. Thus, one determining factor differentiating one model of integration from 
another (at least in the Five views) remains a theological issue related to the doctrine of creation and

title: readings Whitney Article review (Part 5/10)
content:
revelation regarding how much “truth” the different authors believe can be discerned through human 
reason despite the reality of sin—which also has ramifications for one’s understanding of knowledge and 
how this relates to embodiment. For instance, David Myers holds a view of creation and general revela- 
tion that is most open to certain “truths” being discerned through psychology while other positions are 
more cautious about how much truth is discerned through psychology because of theological differences 
regarding general revelation and creation. While the doctrine of creation and revelation inform why the 
authors hold different viewpoints on integration, these theological biases or leanings about revelation and 
creation are not frequently stated, and it would be helpful for authors and teachers of integration to state 
their theological positions on these subjects, since this has ramifications for their particular “view.”

 Whitney

53

While all Christians should be interested in knowing biblical notions of human nature through 
God in Jesus Christ, they may or may not be interested in how creation (and the human creature) 
have an intrinsic logic and order that psychology can help clarify. On the other hand, all psycholo- 
gists will be interested in the patterns of human thought and behavior while they may or may not 
acknowledge that the triune God established these intrinsic orders and patterns. It is left upto the 
integrationist to hold onto both of these notions when practicing the integration of psychology and 
theology. While all evangelicals engaging in faith integration in psychology will say that one’s 
Christian faith needs to shape the practice and discipline of psychology, evangelicals will differ as 
to how much the discipline of psychology can actually clarify, broaden, or inform Christian theol- 
ogy depending in large part on their understanding of the goodness of creation, and the degree to 
which they perceive general and special revelation working together.

Conclusion #2: Human nature can be explored through psychology because God’s 
providential care allows a certain order of creation to be preserved despite the 
reality of sin

God made a physical world and human creatures that have a certain order to them, which in the begin- 
ning God proclaimed as “good.” These orders of creation can be observed through human interaction in 
the world through the sciences and psychology. However, the question remains: How much can we rely 
on the observations that one has about the world and human nature because sin is present? Hasn’t sin 
destroyed one’s ability to discern things accurately? And, hasn’t sin completely cormpted one’s ability 
to perceive truth? These are questions of importance, because, as mentioned above, one’s view of how 
much sin has cormpted human ability to discern truth in the world also determines how much one 
believes that psychology can speak to realities about the human person through measurement, study, and 
evaluation. As mentioned above, one’s theological position concerning creation and how much sin has 
cormpted human’s ability to discern tmth in the world, also determines how much (if any) psychological 
science can inform or broaden aspects of Christian theology. If one believes that psychology can yield 
insights about the human person (and most integrationists do believe this to some degree), then there has 
to be some theological conception of how this can occur even though sin is still present.

One way to make sense of how the order of creation is still intelligible to humans (both to the 
Christian and non-Christian) is explicated through the notion of “common grace” given to all of crea- 
tion. Dutch theologian and statesman, Abraham Kuyper, notes that common grace is the view that God 
gives blessings to both the redeemed and the unredeemed. Common grace helps us make sense of how 
God works in the wider realms of culture outside of the church (which is vital to have some sense of 
when working within psychology). Common grace is God’s movement through the Son and Spirit to 
develop human culture and promote human flourishing even though sin is still present (Kim-van 
Daalen, 2012). God’s goodness of life is bestowed upon both the righteous and unrighteous alike— 
upholding the life of his creatures, and taking no pleasure in the death of even the wicked (Ezekiel 
18:23). God creates, but does not leave creation on its own. He graciously reaches out to creation sus- 
taining and perfecting the created order from destruction. Finally, it is the work of the Holy Spirit to 
restrain the power of sin so that its effects are not as bad as they could be (Kuyper, 2015), as it is also 
the work of the Spirit to actualize human creativity. As H. Richard Niebuhr (1951) reminds us, the 
world is “perverted good, not evil” so that the world is not “bad” in its being (p. 194). Kuyper also 
defined common grace as God’s movement to restrain the power of sin and to develop “a sense of 
domesticity, natural love, the practice of human virtue, the improvement of the public conscience, 
integrity, mutual loyalty among people, and a feeling for piety leaven life” (Kuyper, 2015, p. 181).

There is much connection here with the Wesleyan notion of “prevenient grace.” Both “common 
grace” and “prevenient grace” make sense of how goodness or aspects of tmth are present in the world 
despite the reality of sin; however, the difference exists among the Reformed and Wesleyan tradition in 
how this grace leads to salvation (Cox, 1969, p. 143). For the Reformed tradition common grace is not

 54

Journal of Psychology and Theology 48( I )

salvific, whereas in the Wesleyan tradition prevenient grace is the working in someone’s life that occurs 
before conversion to lead someone to saving grace—while acknowledging that prevenient grace does 
not always have to lead to salvation since it can be resisted. Interestingly enough, both of these theo- 
logical traditions have resources within their tradition for understanding how truth can be discerned 
although sin exists. Sin does disrupt the ordering of God’s creation, within the human heart and in the 
structures and cultures of society—while not ultimately destroying God’s good creation entirely. God 
is committed to both nonhuman and human creation, and this redemption occurs in the person and 
work of Jesus Christ and through the perfecting action of the Spirit (Gunton, 1995).

Common grace establishes how someone who might not be a believer might be able to find 
health and elements of wholeness within this life, even though he or she might not acknowledge 
God. Sin can and does pervert how humanity relates to God, but it does not pervert God’s commit- 
ment to his beloved creation and the human community (Moltmann, 1985, p. 233). The Spirit 
continues to graciously sustain humanity—even those that move against God’s commands. The 
order of creation is not completely destroyed by human sin or evil, and even though it may be dis- 
rupted, God’s covenant love continues to graciously “overrule the worst that the human race can do 
to subvert his order” (Gunton, 1998, p. 192). Since sin does not destroy or corrupt creation entirely, 
and since God remains committed to his creation, then we must have room in our theology to speak 
of the “goodness” that is still present within creation. Additionally, as Calvin noted, for Christ- 
followers one cannot observe the wonder of creation and its goodness without it leading the believer 
back to the worship and glory of the Creator.

If one’s theological leanings focus on the “badness” of the human being so that humans cannot 
discern any of the goodness that God preserves in creation through psychology, the doctrine of 
creation is subordinated to the doctrine of sin. While this focus on the sinful nature of humans is 
understandable, this focus has unfortunately often been interpreted that sin has (1) destroyed all of 
the good within human persons, or (2) destroyed completely humanity’s ability to discern truth or 
reality within the physical world and through the science of psychology.

Conclusion #3: God endows humanity with creative abilities to discover and 
develop the created order through the science of psychology

God’s creation of the human person and placing humanity within creation to tend and care for 
the earth naturally suggests caring or shaping of the created realm through human activity (cul- 
ture). This is essential for those who teach the integration of psychology and theology since 
(theologically speaking) one can and should view psychology as an element of human culture, 
in that it concerns shaping and stewarding the world in a certain way. As has already been estab- 
lished, the order and unity of creation means that there is a world that can be investigated—and 
that truths can be gleaned through the human activity of investigation. Truth can be discovered 
in the world, and the human activity of psychology (and meaning making) shapes the created 
realm in a certain kind of way. Drawing on Kuyper, creation is not only discovered through the 
enterprise of science, but also developed. As Kuyper (1931) states, “And for our relation to the 
world: the recognition that in the whole world the curse is restrained by grace, that the life of 
the world is to be honored in its independence, and that we must, in every domain, discover the 
treasures and develop the potencies hidden by God in nature and in human life” (p. 31). For 
Kuyper, since humans stand in a certain relation with the world, they have the ability to dis- 
cover the treasures of creation, and to develop these patterns found in creation. These two terms 
are helpful points for those practicing integration of psychology and Christianity, since one can 
assume that there are truths to be gleaned from the study of the human creature (discovery), and 
that as humans make meaning of these truths they develop a culture around these discoveries. 
Not only has God made humans naturally curious creatures, but also it makes sense that human- 
ity’s God-given relational capacities would make human creatures intensely curious about other

title: readings Whitney Article review (Part 6/10)
content:
er

 Whitney

55

human creatures. Wolters reappraises this understanding from Kuyper and uses the terms of 
“structure” and “direction” (Wolters, 2005). Creation provides the structure so that the natural 
created world can be discovered, but it is up to the human working in culture to give these dis- 
coveries a direction. For Wolters, the Christian is charged with directing their discoveries to the 
working of the Kingdom, in whatever domain of study that they might find themselves in 
(Wolters, 2005). From the perspective of a trinitarian account of creation, the Spirit is the one 
who particularizes and transforms, and enables things to be what they are intended to be. This 
becomes evident in all realms of culture as humans engage in the creative and investigative 
process to give the creation a voice and shape it in a certain direction. The world’s fundamental 
goodness is established through Christ who became incarnate, and humanity has the ability to 
transform and give direction to the world they are investigating as they are enabled through the 
Holy Spirit. From a theological standpoint it is the Spirit who guides human effort in this crea- 
tive and directive process. There is something good about the created world, and there is some- 
thing equally good when humans express their creative and directive capacities by engaging 
with the world that God has created.

With this in mind, one may understand that the study of the human person through psychology 
is a natural outworking of one way that the created realm can be shaped and studied. Through a 
theological lens, one can understand psychology as simply one way that humans express their curi- 
osity and creativity through the study of human thought and behavior, in addition to making mean- 
ing of the things that one finds from the discovery and study of the human person (Gadamer, 1999). 
Humans are called to not only discover truths about the created order but also to develop these 
patterns that are found in creation—and these capacities of discovery and development are actual- 
ized by the work of the Spirit (Gunton, 1997). For Kuyper (1931), this human action of discovery 
and development has potential to shed “light on the glories of the entire cosmos in its visible phe- 
nomena and operations,” and there is nothing either in the life of nature or in human life that does 
not “present itself as an object worthy of investigation” (p. 126). The importance of upholding the 
goodness and order of the material realm places greater emphasis on how one understands human 
action within the creation. And one also could argue that the discovery and development done 
within psychology concerning human thought and behavior have a special place within the sei- 
enees from a theological perspective since they specifically study the relational and personal 
dimensions of humanity, which, according to Scripture, is central in Christ’s work of redemption.

Humans have a special role within the world of discovering and developing the created realm 
through the sciences, and this is a natural and good use of the gifts that God has given humanity. 
And to press this point further, earlier we discussed how the created realm is knowable but the 
physical world can only speak about God in ambiguous or vague terms (Torrance, 1982). Since the 
created world has an order that speaks, but speaks in vague terms, it is left up to humans as agents 
or stewards of creation to give articulation to the wonders of the human person through the science 
of psychology. According to Scripture, humanity is placed in the garden to shepherd or steward 
over the creation that God has given—with Genesis establishing that God has given tasks for 
humans to carry out in the created world. In the same line of work as naming of the animals, 
humans are given a special role in organizing and classifying what is discovered. This is another 
argument that establishes the value of the discipline of psychology. Humans find their proper place 
not in remaining above creation in some sort of hierarchy that dominates or destroys creation—but 
in recognizing their continuity (or relationality) with the created realm by rightly relating to God, 
others, and nonhuman creation.9 This shaping of the world takes place—whether this occurs

9.  Another improper view of creation among those interpretations ofthe Genesis text takes it to mean that humans 
dominate or stand over the created world in a place of ultimate authority (there is a difference between domin- 
ion and domination). Unfortunately, interpretations ofthe Genesis text that read “dominion” as “domination” 
result in the exploitation of creation for commerce and are seen in how some evangelicals despise creation or 
dismiss it for an otherworldly salvation, as American novelist Wendell has observed (Berry, 1973).

 56

Journal of Psychology and Theology 48( I )

through plowing a farm, painting a work of art, performing research on human behavior, or explor- 
ing human nature and behavior through clinical work. While much of the work among evangelicals 
within integration notes that truth can be discovered through psychology, there is little discussion 
regarding how this discovery of the order of the human creature contributes to God’s mandate of 
developing or fashioning the earth, and giving creation a voice (Begbie, 1997).

The conceptualization that has been sketched so far of the created realm helps give a theological 
vision for how God works in and through the human activity of psychology. If the created realm did 
not have a pattern or order, then the human enterprise of science or psychology could not take place. 
Moreover, if there is a crisis of belief that the created realm is good and something worthy in and of 
itself, this will dramatically impact various human cultural enterprises, and this includes the human 
enterprise of psychology (Gunton, 2002). This understanding of creation outlined also details how 
Christians may learn from non-Christians, so that truth may be discovered and human life informed 
and nourished from sources that are not specifically Christian, that is, by articulating the various 
forms and structures found in creation through psychology. Furthermore, if operating with a theology 
that does not affirm that human discovery in creation is part of a stewardship role to develop the ere- 
ated realm through psychological culture, then it will be more difficult to understand how psychology 
may contribute to our knowledge and understanding of the world. Psychology is exploration into the 
human person and behavior, and it can also be seen theologically as fulfilling our task as human crea- 
tures in exploring, developing, and shaping creation in a particular way. God has created humans with 
the ability to explore, wonder, dream, and develop creation—and psychology is one form in which 
this exploration or development takes place. The created realm presents a reality that has an intrinsic 
logic since it is from a good Creator—truth is accessible in the world through the secular, not because 
it proves the existence of God, but because God has ordered a world that is knowable to the one who 
investigates and explores. Psychology assumes that there are forms or patterns in the natural order 
that can be investigated, and psychological research not only helps discover truths about the created 
realm but also helps develop these truths.

Interaction with the material world is a necessary part of being human—what happens to and within 
the created world ultimately has significance for the ways that we become persons since our physical 
world shapes and orients the cognitive, social, and emotional dimensions of personhood (Brown & 
Strawn, 2012). Since the world is unified and ordered in a certain way, humans have been given the 
ability to investigate and explore the patterns found in creation, and ultimately give articulation to the 
order and patterns that are found regarding human thought and behavior. Humans shape the world in a 
certain way and at the same time human personhood is also shaped by the world that they inhabit 
through their discoveries and development. Additionally, it was noted that these discoveries hold 
potential to be developed and directed in a certain way, and for the Christian, these discoveries are 
offered up for the ultimate glory of God’s Kingdom. While not all theories in psychology entirely align 
with the Christian gospel, one can still conceive psychology as a part of human exploration of the ere- 
ated order that God has established. This brings the discussion to our final conclusions, which is the 
situating of the human creature within the created realm, and is the topic to which we now turn.

Conclusion #4: God’s Trinitarian relations with the created world establish the 
theological basis for the social, embodied, and relational aspects of human nature 
that are discerned through the study of psychology

Enlightenment scientific understandings of creation typically leaned towards Deism (God is 
removed from the workings of the world) so that that God was so separate from the universe that 
he only interacted with the world through natural laws. The natural sciences during the modern era 
touted deistic conceptions of the world that posited a “separateness” of God from the created realm 
so that God only interacted with creation through a series of natural laws. This picture that Deism 
portrays of God is the clockmaker who simply winds the clock and sits back as the world runs its

title: readings Whitney Article review (Part 7/10)
content:
Whitney

57

course with little or no intervention supernaturally. This mechanistic view of the universe resulted 
in several detrimental elements that were eventually incorporated into Western conceptions of the 
world and human person. They include: (1) the loss of the personal aspect of God’s involvement 
with creation; (2) conceptions of the human person that were less holistic and more dualistic; (3) 
the loss of the connection between the human and nonhuman creation.

In the early twentieth century many evangelical Christians rejected deism, but as a result they 
stressed a more biblicist or literal interpretation of the creation narrative. This history of the influ- 
ence of Enlightenment thought upon American evangelicalism has been well documented else- 
where, so those steps need not be retraced here, but the residue of this Enlightenment heritage still 
impinge on integration efforts in psychology and theology.10 This history of the doctrine of creation 
and how God interacts with the natural world does have direct correlations with how evangelical 
Christians approach integration. A thin account of how God operates in the natural world will 
impact conceptions of integration resulting in divisions in how one understands the realms of the 
sacred and secular, with focus on spiritual development as something generally individualistic 
between God and the Christian—instead of Christian spirituality, salvation, and development being 
understood as operating in and with the physical world as the individual is embedded within a com- 
munity (Brown & Strawn, 2012; Wright, 2008).

As mentioned, it is possible to discover truths about creation because God has fashioned a world 
that will disclose its secrets to those who inquire. The development and movement of humanity 
towards wholeness is something that takes place within this created realm, and consequently, 
change and transformation have an inherent social aspect to them. As British theologian John 
Webster observes, true humanity is not a possessed individualistic identity but “rather life in a 
perpetual movement of receiving and responding” to the gifts and the absolute generosity of the 
triune God (Webster, 2003, p. 228; King & Whitney, 2015).

The doctrine of creation offers a theological account of how God’s free relations of love with the 
human and nonhuman creation form the structure for human development and human involvement 
with culture. However, human interactions with the material world are the primary point of study for 
psychology as the Christian psychologist understands that this remains the theological basis for rela- 
tionality and embodiment. A rich account of the doctrine of creation affirms that humans are mutually 
conditioned and constituted by their social environment, and this stands in correspondence with what 
cognitive, developmental, and social psychology have demonstrated (Brosch & Van Bavel, 2012; 
Schwabe & Wolf, 2013). However, the Christian conception of this provides a rich telos that deepens 
our understanding of this plasticity and malleability; All creatures are formed in relationship to others 
(of their own kind or to other species), but human creatures are created with the capacity to be in a 
particular relationship with God and be open to God for transformation. Thus, true humanity is found 
in “participation in the unfolding of God’s ways with humankind” (Webster, 2003, p. 228; King & 
Whitney, 2015). This is also informed by the basic belief that Christ’s Spirit works to bring wholeness 
to the psychological and emotional aspects of personhood. Balanced with the doctrine of creation, this 
movement towards wholeness can occur within the created realm since growth and change happen by 
coming into a restorative relationship with another.

Three ramifications for psychology are found here. First, the relational and embodied aspects of 
humanity speak to the theological conception of embodiment that should undergird developmental 
and social psychology for the Christian. As mentioned earlier, Irenaeus’s case is that Christ’s incar- 
nation legitimizes the value and importance of humanity within the material realm. Irenaeus also 
argues that the incarnation affirms life from infancy to adulthood, and makes the case that every 
aspect of one’s life can be affirmed because Christ aligned himself with humanity. While Jesus did 
not live to an old age (at least to what we might consider old), Irenaeus still argued that all periods 
of human life were sanctified by Christ becoming incarnate. And yet, this point holds importance

10.  See further Noll, 1986; Funkenstein, 1986.

 58

Journal of Psychology and Theology 48( I )

since it is a reminder that all of the interconnected relationships and developing systems which 
humans find themselves embedded in are established through the doctrine of creation, sanctified by 
Christ becoming incarnate, and directed to a certain telos through the Spirit. God has given some 
degree of order to these interconnected dynamic systems that humans find themselves in, and it is 
these features of the created realm that psychology seeks to study and address. It is not only that the 
created realm provides the structure that is able to be studied, but it is also this particular realm that 
we inhabit which shapes our emotional, neurological, and social development. Given the malleabil- 
ity of our personhood and the dynamic work of the Spirit, it compels us continue to develop a theo- 
logical conception of what thriving or flourishing looks like—knowing that a Christian ontology 
also provides notions of becoming and personhood that psychology cannot provide alone (Balswick, 
King, & Reimer, 2016; King & Whitney, 2015; Lemer, 2002).

Second, the movement of God of working towards restoration does not just mean wholeness 
between others and God, but, as Anthony Hoekema observes, becoming a new creation also means 
having a renewed relationship with one’s own self (Hoekema, 1986). Surprisingly, theological 
exposition of this seems to be neglected in much of the integration literature—and represents an 
important topic for further research and reflection (especially for clinical work). The work of the 
Spirit not only gives us greater capacity for relating to others, but also has capacity to change our 
own self-image—and for the Christian, we believe it does, in order to conform us to make us more 
like the image of Christ. This is the dynamic working of the Spirit bringing about a certain telos of 
becoming that occurs through development over the course of one’s life (Schnitker, King, & 
Houltberg, 2019; King, 2016). Thus, with the redemption of the created realm there is also the 
renewal of our how we view ourselves. Creation establishes that as human creatures we are rela- 
tional beings that exist in relationship with God, others, and the nonhuman creation. However, to 
be human also means that we have some degree of relationship with our own selves. As Calvin has 
noted, there is a reciprocal relationship between knowledge of God and knowledge of one’s own 
self.11 Self-perception and understanding of one’s self is made possible because of the inherent 
relational dimension of the created realm. Hoekema argues that one’s self-image is often “either 
inordinately high (in the form of sinful pride) or excessively low (in the form of feelings of shame 
or worthlessness)” (Hoekema, 1986, p. 105). However, as Scripture reminds us, there is already a 
degree of respect and care for one’s own self that is assumed in the command to love our neighbor 
as ourselves (Matthew 22:39). Love for neighbor assumes that there is also some degree of healthy 
self-love that is present—and self-love that honors who God has made us to be is different than 
selfishness. Therefore, transformation through the Spirit not only means that we treat others in a 
new way, but also for the Christian that we treat ourselves in a way that is commensurate with the 
reality that there is no condemnation in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1).

Third, the social, embodied, and relational aspects of human nature outlined by a trinitarian 
understanding of creation bear on both the method and practice of integration between psychology 
and theology (Brown & Strawn, 2012; Sandage & Brown, 2015). As mentioned above, modernity’s 
influence in the West resulted in conceptions of the human person that were less holistic and more 
dualistic, and had the tendency to separate mind from body. These tendencies are still present 
within evangelicalism, and also within some integration efforts that divorce information or knowl- 
edge from embodiment. Knowledge, in the biblical perspective, is not solely objective or informa- 
tion-based; it is relationally grounded and mediated. If knowledge is relationally meditated, then 
observation always moves one into relationship with the subject that is being observed and is never 
something that can be done in complete objectivity—as the scientific method often implies 
(Polyani, 1962). A danger in teaching integration between psychology and Christianity arises when

11.  Calvin famously said that “Nearly all the wisdom we possess, that is to say, true and sound wisdom, 
consists of two parts: the knowledge of God and of ourselves. But, while joined by many bonds, which 
one precedes and which brings forth the other is not easy to discern” (Calvin, 1960, p. 35).

 Whitney

59

integration is portrayed to students as something that is simply theoretical apart from the embodied 
and ethical components of human personhood. Moreover, as Sandage and Brown (2018) correctly 
note, the work of integration does not just occur on the level of abstract ideas, but involves whole 
and embodied persons to do the work of integration. Stressing in our teaching that integration 
involves understanding one’s social and cultural location for how one embodies the world, how this 
impacts theological reflection, and how this bears on one’s study of psychology is not only impor- 
tant but essential for the integration of one’s own self, and the development of the ethical dimen- 
sions of personhood (Sandage & Brown, 2015).

title: readings Whitney Article review (Part 8/10)
content:
5).

Implications for Psychological Research and Psychotherapy

The ramifications of what has been outlined above move beyond simply establishing the theologi- 
cal foundations for how the doctrine of creation impacts integration to also providing insight for 
clinical work, research, becoming a spiritually integrated person, and teaching psychology in the 
undergraduate and graduate setting. While all of these implications cannot be discussed here, the 
burden of this article has been to open the conversation and dialogue for how a trinitarian doctrine 
of creation impacts our understanding of integration. Having established the basic framework of 
how creation provides some valuable ways to approach integration, I propose, by way of conclu- 
sion, to further sketch how some of these notions might have implications for psychology in the 
realm of clinical work and some aspects of psychological research.

As Christ-followers who are psychologists, one of our roles is to join with the work of God in 
redeeming patterns of human behavior by helping folks understand how they relate to them- 
selves, others, and the world around them either through psychotherapy or through research. Or 
to use “Kuyperian” language, the work of psychotherapy can help persons discover new things 
about themselves and develop these patterns in a direction that is helpful. In many cases, the 
relational patterns that we are unaware of often are the patterns that cause the greatest difficulties 
in one’s relationships. With this in mind, we might understand the work of therapy as helping 
patients to understand and discover how patterns of the past causes one (often unknowingly) to 
act certain ways in their relationships and even towards one’s own self. Taking what we estab- 
lished above regarding general revelation and common grace, we can acknowledge that these 
relational patterns can be discovered simply because God has made a universe and human crea- 
tures that have discernable orders and patterns. Once these relational patterns are discovered, the 
psychologist can work to develop these patterns of behavior towards wholeness—with particular 
focus on changing negative relational patterns. Negative patterns of relating are usually rooted 
in real pain or hurt from the past, and fortunately some of these patterns are also discernable 
through human relationship and inquiry. If we want to use theological language, our negative 
patterns of relating do come through our sinfulness but also as a result of us being sinned against. 
God’s Spirit works through clinicians to bring wholeness and healing through the discovery of 
relational patterns. Moreover, we must also acknowledge that the Spirit of God is at work and 
leads those who are children of God (Romans 8:9-10; 8:14), and we can trust that this is happen- 
ing as we commit clinical work to Christ. The development and movement of humanity towards 
wholeness is something that takes place within this created realm, between the clinician and cli- 
ent, which also demonstrates how change and transformation have an inherent social aspect 
(Sandage & Brown, 2018).

Common grace enables us to see that God can work positively in the field of psychology in ways 
that benefit humanity—especially seen in, but not limited to, those areas of psychology that work to 
promote the health and well-being of families, couples, and children. Thus, common grace is at work 
in the family therapist who earnestly helps parents reconcile in order to provide a safer home envi- 
ronment for their children. Common grace enables us to see how the clinician who works with those 
who struggle with a severe mental illness have been graciously given gifts to care for those who are

 60

Journal of Psychology and Theology 48( I )

often overlooked in our society. These gifts of God’s common grace do not bring salvation, but they 
are graciously bestowed upon people so that God’s goodness may shine forth. They are glimpses and 
reminders of God’s coming Kingdom—a Kingdom where persons are whole and complete human 
beings. The doctrine of creation and providence reminds us that God is committed to sustaining and 
upholding his creation, and common grace helps explicate how this might take place.

Agreeing with Charles Taylor, the individualistic and isolated Western notions of the self and 
personhood have resulted in depersonalization and disengagement within wider society—and 
unfortunately these have affected psychological theory and some streams of evangelicalism. Deism 
had the tendency to devalue the doctrine of creation so that both persons and societies became more 
disengaged from one another, and disengagement can be understood as standing apart from others 
and the world in a depersonalized fashion so that the “other” is treated as an object (Gunton, 2002). 
A neglect of the trinitarian doctrine of creation causes one to lose the interconnected nature that 
humans have with others, God, and even nonhuman creation. Thus, the outcome of modernity was 
an essentially rationalist conception of the human being and the world, that humans are essentially 
a mind. If we are only a mind, then we discern the world divorced from body and emotions. In 
short, the way that we are fashioned into the people we are supposed to be happens (or doesn’t hap- 
pen) through the ways that our personal relationships are ordered (this is also where our ecclesiol- 
ogy comes in) since the community of the church is the realm where such social transformation 
takes place to form a new community that loves and worships Jesus Christ. One way that findings 
in social and cognitive psychology help push or inform our understanding of creation is demon- 
strating and confirming just how malleable we are as humans in regards to our behavior, how con- 
nected and influenced we are by social realities, and how prone to bias we are due to our cognitive 
schemas that are shaped by social identities (Bartlett, 1932; Schacter, Norman, & Koutstaal, 1998; 
Bernstein, Sacco, Young, & Hugenberg, 2014). Given the prevalence of how social realities shape 
our view of others, our behavior represents one future direction that a theology of creation must 
continue to engage with since it reinforces the importance of the role that the church has in shaping 
and creating new social realities (Brown & Strawn, 2012). Historically, through much of modern 
Western society the personal and communal dimensions of human personhood have been over- 
looked and humanity’s relation with the world neglected However, this trend is counteracted 
through resources found in a trinitarian conception of creation that stresses the relational, social, 
and embodied dimensions of personhood.

Psychology represents a certain way that humans explore the order and unity of creation by 
discovering, giving voice to, and classifying information regarding human behavior, biology, and 
nature. Humans inherently make meaning of this information as irreducibly hermeneutic creatures 
(Gadamer, 1999), and this meaning making is also part of humanity’s God-given relational and 
creative capacities. Informed with a doctrine of creation that upholds the value of the material crea- 
tion and sees the connection between creature and creation, one begins to see how, from a theologi- 
cal perspective, the field of psychological science shapes the culture as one explores and investigates 
the created human person. While the work through psychotherapy does not bring salvation, it 
nevertheless can be viewed as part of God’s processes to bring wholeness to his creation. 
Consequently, Christians working within psychology are part of shaping the realm that God has 
given us with the view that God is bringing those we work with towards wholeness. God’s love 
reaches far beyond the bounds of only the “elect”—it reaches out to the whole world desiring that 
all people, everywhere come to know greater wholeness and abundant life. Job 34:14 reminds us 
that if God withdrew his Spirit, then all of humanity would perish. God as creator sustains his good 
creation, speaking his continual “yes” to life as he upholds the human creature and creation from 
dissolution (Moltmann, 1985).

This also means that Christians who work in psychological research and/or practice psycho- 
therapy have a part to play in extending this love and working for change, and this change often 
times manifests itself in the relational work that takes place as one interacts with others and

 Whitney

61

provides research that can give a “roadmap” for how to order one’s personal relationships. As 
Christians, we understand that God desires life for his creation, and this involves the prospering 
and flourishing of human life. It is the personal relations that we have with ourselves, others, and 
God (and even nonhuman creation) that shape who we are. Moreover, the doctrine of creation 
informs how God’s free relations of love with the human and nonhuman creation form the basis for 
human development and human involvement with culture (King & Whitney, 2015; Balswick, 
King, & Reimer, 2016). In this way, clinicians can be seen, in part, as helping to reorder the re la- 
tionships and patterns of behavior through the social and relational aspects of something like psy- 
chotherapy or research. We have both sinned and been sinned against, which disrupts the normal 
patterns and order of relating with other

title: readings Whitney Article review (Part 9/10)
content:
s. The material created realm is where humanity has suf- 
fered from sin, but creation also represents the stage where redemption begins and occurs. Clinical 
work, in part, seeks to help patients grow and change through a reparative relationship—and this is 
discernable because of the vision of creation that we have established, i.e. God has made these 
relational patterns and orders known and discernable. It has also been argued that psychology is 
unique and of value with its focus on the relational and behavioral since the realm that Christ came 
to redeem is the relational dimension of one’s personhood. This emphasis on the relational nature 
of humanity set within the arc of the biblical narrative of creation, redemption, and restoration has 
also caused scholars to ask questions about what it means to flourish or thrive from theological and 
psychological perspectives, i.e. what is a meaningful and purposeful end to existing as a human? 
(McMinn, 2017; Balswick, King, & Reimer 2016; Volf & Crisp, 2015; Charry, 2010). The prosper- 
ing and flourishing of human life is what Jesus’ ministry of reconciliation is about, and working for 
God’s Kingdom here on earth means working towards shalom, which is not merely an absence of 
conflict but is a fullness or wholeness to life. In effect, we can understand the reign of God to be a 
reordering and restoration of creation that has been disrupted by sin. Shalom is the wholeness that 
God intended for his creation and creatures, and, balanced with an understanding of the doctrine of 
creation, we can understand that humans have a role in tending, caring for, and being good stewards 
of the small part of the created realm that we embody as persons.

Concluding Remarks

This article has proposed a theological evaluation of the nature and work of psychology that is 
informed by the trinitarian doctrine of creation, and argued that the resurgence of the doctrine of 
creation in theological circles has also opened up new possibilities for how we conceive the rela- 
tionship between theology and psychology. A thin account of the doctrine of creation limits the 
scope of creation to the first chapter of Genesis and neglects how it is effectively related to other 
doctrines (like revelation and common grace). When this is done the doctrine of sin is often ele- 
vated to a place of importance over the doctrine of creation so that the goodness of the created order 
and creature are neglected or even denigrated. Conversely, situating the doctrine of creation within 
the larger arc of the biblical narrative emphasizes the goodness of the created world and speaks to 
how God reveals Godself to the human creature. A trinitarian understanding of creation highlights 
the personal and communal nature of humanity and humans’ relationship with the material realm 
that is of great value for integration efforts in psychology—and especially for establishing that the 
practice of integration includes integration within persons, and not merely the integration of con- 
cepts or ideas (Sandage & Brown, 2018).

A doctrine of creation that includes the notion of common grace gives clarity to how God’s Spirit 
brings forth good things through psychology, whether or not a particular psychologist acknowledges 
God. Common grace is a valuable theological concept for integration since it establishes how one 
understands psychological discoveries made by non-Christians. Is all of psychology “fallen” and in 
need of a pristine “Christian” approach? Or, is God at work in the field of psychology in ways that 
benefit humanity—even though all psychologists might not explicitly acknowledge God? The

 62

Journal of Psychology and Theology 48( I )

argument outlined in this article favors the latter view, and has demonstrated how one’s understanding 
of creation and revelation dictates various positions within the integration dialogue.

While psychology is the exploration of human thought and behavior, it can also be seen theo- 
logically as one particular way humans can explore, develop, and shape creation in particular 
ways—recognizing that one’s ability to explore creation through psychology relies on capacities 
given by God.

One can affirm that psychology is a science—and yet, for the Christian, it is also the activity of 
humans engaging in the process of discovering and exploring God’s world. The more central con- 
cern for those practicing integration is the acknowledgement that through God’s general revelation, 
the world and the human creature have an order that may be discerned by observations through the 
psychological sciences. This revelation may not directly reveal things about God as Redeemer, but 
this general revelation reveals something of the manner and order of the way that God has put 
together the created realm and the human creature. As mentioned above, this does not mean that all 
of this exploration and observation aligns with all Christian convictions, and yet, we can still 
acknowledge that the role of discovering and developing and making meaning of God’s world is 
something that is part of simply being human.

The significance that this holds for psychology cannot be overstated. Just as common grace 
allows us to makes sense of the goodness found in creation, there are elements of truth that are 
worth discovering within and about the human creature. Kuyper, for example, argues that there are 
features within human life that are “worthy of investigation.” Since the world and human creatures 
have patterns that are intrinsic to their existence, it follows that these patterns ( especially of human 
behavior) are worth exploring.

It is possible to see the work of psychology, and more specifically the integration of Christian 
theology and psychology, as finding and developing these areas of “truth” that people possess and 
helping them understand their own patterns of behavior. It is possible to discover truths about crea- 
tion because God has fashioned a world that will disclose its secrets to those who inquire. Moreover, 
the Christian experience is informed by the basic belief that Christ’s Spirit works to bring whole- 
ness to the psychological and emotional aspects of our person—and informed by a trinitarian 
understanding of creation—aspects of this wholeness can occur within the created realm. Moreover, 
growth and change happen best, both behaviorally and spiritually, in the context of healing rela- 
tionships. Since God created a world that is knowable to the one who inquires, Christian psycholo- 
gists can see part of their work as helping others to understand their own relational patterns and 
develop these to bring people into reconciliation with themselves and others.

sectors.

title: Slides: COUN 5773 - Appendix D - In-Person
content:
Theology and Counseling
COUN 5773
Appendix D
Instructions for Faith and Counseling Integration Paper (30%)
Students will compose a 2500-5000 word paper which includes at least three major sections developing the
following areas, with an appropriate introduction and conclusion. The paper should also provide an abstract
of no more than 100 words at the front and a bibliography at the back, which can be included in the all-
inclusive word count.
The student is to use at least six (6) scholarly and peer-reviewed sources beyond the assigned readings
from the disciplines of psychology, philosophy, theology, sociology, or other related disciplines.
1) A Theology-Philosophy of the Human Self
The student is to provide a research-based account of their own theology and/or philosophy of the human
being, of the self. This can include a wide range of emphases, and choosing the most salient topics for
research and presenting an account of the human is part of the task of this paper. Students may utilize
course-assigned materials in research as well as their minimum of six (6) additional sources to compose
this portion of the paper.
2) A Moral Basis for a Social Ethic in Relationship to the Student’s Vocation
The second part of the paper is a moral theology and/or philosophy and social ethic related to the student’s
intended vocation in some area of counseling psychology. Thus the student is to make connections between
the student’s theological and/or philosophical anthropology, morality, social ethics, and the student’s
vocation. This portion of the paper should include research into these moral-ethical dynamics involved in
vocational practices which are related to certain convictions regarding human beings.
3) Area of Advocacy and Justice
The third part of the papers should identify an area of advocacy which can be addressed through the
student’s intended vocational work. This part of the paper will develop an approach that will envision areas
of enaction of the student’s moral philosophy or social ethic or convictions about justice, considering
possible ways this might be practiced in the student’s vocational calling.

-- 1 of 4 --

Course Learning Outcomes for this Assignment: CLO 5: Construct an integrative research paper that
develops faith and philosophical sources in an integration with counseling ethics, justice, and advocacy
(CACREP Standard 2b, 2d, 2g, and 2g).

-- 2 of 4 --

title: Slides: COUN 5773 Theology and CounselingMF - 2026
content:
College of Social and Behavioral Sciences 
Master of Arts in Clinical Mental Health Counseling 
COUN 5773 Theology and Counseling 
Spring 2026
nstructor:  
Marty Folsom, Ph.D. 
      NU E-mail: Marty.Folsom@northwestu.edu 
          Personal E-mail:  drtrinity@comcast.net 
          Phone: 425-3434-494 
 
Office Hours:   
By appointment only, in-person, Zoom, or Messenger Video Chat 
 
Class Schedule: 
January 6 (Tu), January 20 (Tu), February 3 (Tu), February 17 (Tu), March 3 (Tu), March 17 (Tu), March 31 (Tu), April 14 (Tu), 4 hours weeklyRequired Text:  
Anderson, Ray S. (2010), Christians who Counsel: The Vocation of Wholistic Therapy. Wipf & Stock. 
Callaway, Kutter, and Whitney, William B. (2022). Theology for Psychology and Counseling: An Invitation to Holistic Christian Practice. Baker Academic.
. 
Course Description:  
 This course presents a Christian theological perspective and encourages the student to think deeply about their own beliefs about the nature of humans in light of contemporary issues in the field of counseling. 
 
Course Objectives:  
The student will: 
Compose a self-narrative that identifies religious, spiritual, and ethical self-understanding in relation to the vocation of mental health counseling (CACREP Standard 2b, 2d, and 2g). 
Assess anthropological assumptions in religious and philosophical texts (CACREP Standard 2a, 2b, 2d, 2g, and 2h). 
Summarize several written communications on the integration of faith and mental health counseling for use by peers (CACREP Standard 2d and 2g). 
Construct an integrative research paper that develops faith and philosophical sources in an integration with counseling ethics, justice, and advocacy (CACREP Standard 2b, 2d, 2g, and 2g). 
 
CACREP Standards:  
 2F.2.a. multicultural and pluralistic characteristics within and among diverse groups nationally and internationally 
 2F.2.b. theories and models of multicultural counseling, cultural identity development, and social justice and advocacy 
 2F.2.d. the impact of heritage, attitudes, beliefs, understandings, and acculturative experiences on an individual's views of others 
 2F.2.g. the impact of spiritual beliefs on clients' and counselors' worldviews  2F.2.h. strategies for identifying and eliminating barriers, prejudices, and processes of intentional and unintentional oppression and discrimination

Academic Honesty: 
Northwest University expects honesty from students in all areas, including their academic lives. Academic dishonesty is a serious violation of University standards and may result in the students' receiving an "F" on the assignment, receiving an "F" for the course, or possibly being dismissed from the University. Instances of academic dishonesty are coordinated through the respective graduate program office and typically reported to the Provost's Office. 
Academic dishonesty includes cheating on assignments or examinations, submitting the same (or essentially the same) paper in more than one course without prior consent of the current assigning professor(s), sabotaging another student's work, and plagiarizing. Plagiarism is "using someone else's ideas or phrasing and representing those ideas or phrasing as our own, either on purpose or through carelessness" (MLA).  Any student disciplined by a faculty member for alleged academic dishonesty has the right to appeal the disciplinary action. The student should initiate his/her appeal according to procedures outlined in the Northwest University Student Academic Appeals Policy, which is available in the Provost Office.  
 
Attendance Policy:   
Class attendance is extremely important for student learning and achievement of specified course outcomes and, in turn, the program aims. Hence, attendance at all class sessions is expected. At minimum, students absent for a class session will need to make up any missed assignments or exercises and complete the expected readings.  A grade penalty will be applied if more than one class session is missed. Additional policies and consequences are at the discretion of the individual professor of the course. More than one missed class session will result in failure of the course and require a retake to continue in the program. In this circumstance, the student is encouraged to withdraw from the course and retake the class when it is offered again. In the event of unusual circumstances (e.g., extended illness, emergency), a student may appeal the automatic course failure. A proposal outlining a plan for satisfactory course completion will be required, composed by the professor and agreed to by the student. In addition, the plan will require approval by the program director and Dean. 
 
Disability Accommodations and Procedures:   
For students with learning differences and/or physical disabilities, as defined by the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, Northwest University takes an individual and holistic approach to providing accommodations. Accommodations are developed and approved on an individual basis by the Student Development Office, not the CMHC program or individual faculty members. Students needing accommodations for a diagnosed disability should follow the procedures provided on the Student Development Office's website for disability accommodations.  
 
Communication Policy: 
Timely communication between the professor and students is important to the overall functioning of the program. Therefore, both students and faculty should respond to email or phone messages within two business days. If the professor is out-of-office, he or she should have an automatic reply message (on email) or voicemail recording (phone) providing the date of his/her return, as well as an in-office contact for urgent requests. 
 
Grading Policy: 
Grading is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to monitor their progress accordingly. Assignments and tests will generally be graded within two weeks. Final papers and projects may take up to three weeks. The professor will let you know if there are exceptional circumstances preventing a timely return of grades. If you have concerns about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator, or program Chair. 
 
Late Work Policy: 
It is the student's responsibility to meet all deadlines for class assignments.  Grades for each late assignment submitted may be penalized by 20%. It is up to the instructor whether they will accept late work. 
 
Mechanics of the Course: 
 
A) Class Sessions 
Class sessions will be used for a variety of purposes, including:  
Lecture 
Class and Group Discussion 
Writing 
B) Response, Research, or Other Written Assignments  
 
Discussion Boards (20%)   
  
The weekly discussion boards, guided by a question prompt, require a 250-plus word evaluative response from the student. Students will be graded according to their response and will not be required to respond to others. Feedback should be constructive and on topic only, even and especially when challenging the ideas of others. However, the main purpose of the discussion board is for students to provide an evaluative response to a topic to develop evaluations of topics in our subject matter and development in quality of written communication. (Due on the Sunday following class by midnight) 
 
Vocational-Spiritual Self-Narrative (10% of course grade):   
 
Who am I? Who am I in the present, and who have I been? Who am I in my social relations? Who am I in my vocational calling? Who am I in relationship to God (or my conception of ultimate reality)? Who am I as a person studying mental health counseling as a next step in the journey of my calling in life? 
  
Written communication: A 1000-1500 word narrative essay that a) is carefully composed with no or almost no writing errors; b) provides response to the above questions, though the structure of how may vary; c) demonstrates self-understanding and respect for others; d) responds to some of the spiritual, theological, and philosophical notions found in Smith and other assigned course readings, along with other valuable sources at the writer's discretion; and e) APA-formatted citations as necessary.  (Due on February 22)
   
Reading/Article Review (20%) 
 
Construct a 1000-1500-word response to the constructive ideas found in an integrative reading or article of approximately 20-40 pages of scholarly material that engages the intersection of theology and counseling. Students may choose from the options below or propose another option to the professor in the form of an e-mail request with explanation as to why the proposed article is a good candidate for review. (Due on March 8) 
 
Several representative and preapproved options (posted as PDFs in Discovery) include: 
 
Augustine, D. (2019). "Equality, Justice, and Peace." The Spirit and the common good: Shared flourishing in the image of God (pp. 108-120). Eerdmans.  
Balswick, J. O., Balswick, J. K., and Frederick T. V. (2021) “Part 1: Theological and Social Perspectives on Family Life.” In The Family : A Christian Perspective on the Contemporary Home (pp 21-64). Baker.
 
Buber, M. (1958). I-Thou (R.G. Smith, trans.) (pp. 3-23). Scribner.  
 
Dreyfus, H. and Taylor, C. (2015). "Fusing Horizons," In Retrieving Realism (pp. 102-130). Harvard University Press.  
Folsom, M. (2004). "Recursion: A Theological Axiom for Relationships," Marriage & Family: A Christian Journal 7:3,  199-214.
 
McGrath, A. (2017). Human Nature, Sin, and Grace. In Christian Theology (pp. 327-353).  Wiley-Blackwell.  
 
Murphy, N. (2018). Christian Anthropology, Philosophy, and Science. In A Philosophy of the Christian Religion (pp. 210-251). Westminster John Knox.  
  
Rennebohm, S. & Thoburn, J. (2021). Incarnational psychotherapy: Christ as the ground 
of being for integrating psychology and theology. Pastoral Psychology 70, 179-190.
Smith, J.K.A. (2016). "You are what you love: to worship is human." In You are what you love: the spiritual power of habit (pp. 1-25). Brazos.Stearns, C. (2025). Trauma-Informed Christology: ‘Healing Happens “After no Body Becomes Some Body”’.” In Christ & Trauma: Theology East of Eden (pp. 169-189). Wipf & Stock. 
John Swinton (2001) “The Neglect of the Spiritual.” In Spirituality and Mental Health Care : Rediscovering a 'Forgotten' Dimension (pp. 40-63). Jessica Kingsley Publishers.
Whitney, W. B. (2020) “Beginnings: Why the Doctrine of Creation Matters for the Integration of Psychology and Christianity.” Journal of Theology and Psychology Vol. 48 (1) 44-65.
 
Faith and Counseling Integration Paper (40%)  
  
A 2000-3000-word paper which includes at least three major sections, developing the following areas:   
  
Construct a researched description of several theological and/or philosophical affirmations you hold regarding the human person or human communities.  
  
Develop a moral basis for an ethical approach to mental health counseling in relation to your researched description above. 
  
Describe an area of advocacy in which you envision practically applying the above ethical approach to a concrete area of justice or service.  
  
The paper should include six (6) or more scholarly sources identified through the student's own research. (Due April 19) 
 
Field Work: Anthropological Observation (10%) 
 
A 750-1000-word observational paper with attendant documentation observing embedded anthropologies in a socio-cultural setting or a person or persons in their relational setting.  Choose one. (Due on April 5) 
Options A or B will be based on "A Phenomenology of Cultural Liturgies" by J.K.A. Smith. 
Option A: A Christian or another worship observation on the human self before God and with others in religious communities  
Option B: A common or secular practice, anthropological observation on understandings of the human self.
Options C or D will be based on the Integration tools from class discussions.
Option C: Relational EKG - Interview 2 people to assess their positive and negative relations to persons, places, and activities.
Option D: Relational X-Ray – Interview two people on their relational contexts, movements, and progress toward healthy relating.
C) Participation  
A portion of the student's final grade will be based on their participation in class and group discussions, in class exercises, group discussions, and attendance. Full points will be rewarded to those students who attend all class sessions and actively participate in class activities. 
 
D) Grading  
Exams and presentations will be graded the same day. Papers will be graded within 2 weeks. If you have concerns about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator, or program Chair. 
The students course grade will be based upon her/his mastery of course terms, concept, 
and objections as demonstrated by classroom attendance, participation, presentations, and papers. 
Please be on time with all assignments; if late, there will be a deduction of grade points.  Grading is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to monitor their progress.

title: Slides: Field Observation Instructions and Rubric - Spring 2024
content:
Theology and Counseling (COUN 5773)
Spring 2024
Instructions for Field Work: Anthropological Observations (10% each/x2)
Field experience takes place through observations of the anthropologies embedded in one of two
settings: 1) in a setting of Christian worship or another site of religious practice and 2) another
event or setting which identifies an anthropology embedded in a common socio-cultural site or
practice. These should range from between 750-1000 words.
The assignment addresses the following (in an approximately 80% observation/interpretation
and 20% evaluative proportion):
 What is the underlying (implicit) anthropology (understanding of the human) in this
event?
 What is the human (according to the underlying assumptions)?
 What are expectations of the persons involved in this event and how do these
expectations reflect the norms and underlying ideas about humanity which this
community or social situation or event assumes?
 What does this event say about human purposes?
 What is going on that is habituating people, leading them into a way of life in this
event?
 And, briefly, how do you evaluate what you have observed and interpreted here?
The concept behind this assignment is that practices, habits, “liturgies” embed or teach or habituate
understandings of the human into human beings, so that they teach us what the human is through
our doing and being and becoming.
What does the event that you observed do to form humans?

-- 1 of 3 --

Additional Prompts for Anthropological Observations
Who are the human beings in this event expected to be?
What are some of the roles that are being played? How might some of these roles relate to the
whole lives of the persons involved?
What does this event assume about the purposes for the human beings involved in it? What does
this event assume about the purposes of other persons?
What does the naming of people have to do with the understanding of the humans involved in this
event?
What do the human power structures involved in this event look like? What is assumed? What
might be contested?
What is the “unthought” going on about the humans involved in this event and humans in general?
That is, what is so deeply assumed here that it might be an epiphany to recognize as a powerful
assumption guiding this event?
How might this event relate to the larger life of this community in which the event is taking place?
What are the explicit beliefs that are espoused during this event? What are some beliefs that seem
to be implicit in it and the community?
What seems to give meaning to this event? How does that meaning resonate with the participants,
or not?
What are some of the ethical structures embedded in this event and, as you might infer from your
observation, perhaps the wider community? How might the personal moralities of the persons
involved in the event or in the community relate to these ethical structures?
Was there anything odd or surprising to you about the expectations of those involved in this event?
Did you find things to be moral or immoral, ethical or unethical?
Did you find things to be spiritually enriching or problematic or harmful or anything else along
these lines?
What kind of humans is this event shaping those involved in it to be?

-- 2 of 3 --
