title: Slides: Incarnational Psychotherapy - Rennebohm and Thoburn (2)
content:
Vol.:(0123456789)
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11089-021-00940-x
1 3
Incarnational Psychotherapy: Christ as the Ground of Being
for Integrating Psychology and Theology
Samuel B. Rennebohm1 · John Thoburn1
Accepted: 22 January 2021
© The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC part of Springer Nature 2021
Abstract
The integration of psychology and theology is often sought as a layering of intellectual
abstractions from biblical and psychological sources. Though scriptural and scientific data
may partially define the reality of God and human psychology, the Truth is an embodied
act, God made incarnate among us as Christ. The common ground for integration of psy-
chology and theology must then be this ground of being, the incarnate presence of Jesus
Christ among us. The basis for integration is derived theologically from the relational
nature of and God and the ongoing revelation of Christ to humankind through incarnational
presence (i.e., social theology) and psychologically from the preeminence of relationships
in shaping human development, well-being, and psychological distress (i.e., interpersonal
psychology). This paper reviews both traditions in turn before then discussing the incarna-
tion of Christ as the ground of being for their integration. Incarnational relational enact-
ment is proposed to characterize the transformative presence of Christ in the healing work
of a therapeutic relationship. This process is described as immediate, personal, uncondi-
tional, and dialectical, ultimately leading to a literal renewing of the mind.
Keywords Incarnational relational enactment · Psychotherapy process · Integration · Social
theology · Interpersonal psychology · Corrective emotional experience
This article provides an incarnational model of psychotherapy, integrating social theology
with interpersonal psychology. It is our thesis that while biblical and psychological truths
provide context for the integration of psychology and Christian theology, only the incarna-
tion of Christ fully reflects the content of integration. The incarnation of Christ is aroused
through the dialogical therapy relationship, one exemplar of the kenotic community, where
Christ is made manifest in the in-between of the client-therapist relationship. The client
and therapist are ushered into the presence of God, and the relationship takes on quali-
ties that reflect the zeitgeist of Christ: immediate, personal, accepting, and dialectical. This
incarnational relational enactment leads to a corrective emotional experience (both punc-
tuated experiences and ongoing process) for the client, which in turn leads to neuro- and
* Samuel B. Rennebohm
rennebohms@spu.edu
1 	Seattle Pacific University, 3307 3rd Ave W, Seattle, WA 98119, USA
/ Published online: 11 February 2021
Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 1 of 13 --

1 3
synaptogenesis, going beyond cognitive, behavioral, and affective change to a true biopsy-
chosocial/spiritual transformation for the client.
Stevenson et al. (2007) define the integration of psychology and Christianity as “the
task of searching for the unity of truth, seeing things whole from a Christian perspec-
tive, and holistically attempting to relate elements of an objective real world given by
God at creation” (p. 5). Integration, from their perspective, is the search for a renewed
sense of truth through a unity of truth that emerges from combining the truths gleaned
from biblical and revelational sources with the truths discovered in psychological and
relational sources. The resultant phenomenological understanding is necessarily greater
than the sum of its constituent psychological and theological parts.
There can, however, be a problem with a focus on unity of truth if that unity is sought
as a layering of intellectual abstractions viewed apart from the ongoing, relational reve-
lation of Christ. Abstract biblical or psychological assertions become trivialized as fixed
datapoints and only acquire meaning through a detached process of intellectual interpre-
tation. Truth is considered only as the sum of these constituent data parts, without an
experiential understanding of the gestalt through which they are integrated. Truth apart
from the ongoing revelation of Christ reduces integration to a focus on abstract princi-
ples, and truths become little more than a sandwiching together of contemporary under-
standings of biblical and psychological concepts, data, and principles—more a parallel
process than the discovery of common ground.
Benner’s (2007) understanding of integration as incarnational metaphor and Farns-
worth’s (1985) idea of living as Jesus lived move us closer to an understanding of a
Christocentric approach to integration, but we need to recognize that “Jesus did not
become incarnate by embodying truths of an abstract or intellectual form. Rather his
very being is the truth” (Anderson 1990, p. 187). While the context for truth might be
biblical revelation and psychological principles, data, etc., the content of truth is bound
up in Jesus Christ as very man and very God. Tillich (1952) asserted that the essence
of Christ cannot be attributed to the model of behavior that he exemplified, his recorded
teachings, or the written gospels in which these appear. Instead, Tillich suggested that
the essence of Christ is his very being, the relational enactment of Christ among human-
ity. It is from this emphasis on the content of being that we read Jesus’ statement that “I
am the way and the truth and the light” (John 14:6 NRSV). Though there are scriptural
and historical truths that may partially define the reality of God, the Truth is an embod-
ied act, God made incarnate among us as Christ. The common ground for the integration
of psychology and theology must then be this ground of being, the incarnate presence of
Jesus Christ among us, and the ongoing revelation that we experience in relation to him.
To fully articulate the centrality of Christ’s incarnate being to psychological-theo-
logical integration, we will draw on both social theology and interpersonal psychology.
Social theology is premised on the relational nature of the triune God and argues for the
ongoing revelation of Christ to humankind through his incarnational presence. Interper-
sonal psychology focuses on the central role of relationships in shaping human develop-
ment, well-being, and psychological distress. We will begin by reviewing each of these
traditions in turn before discussing the incarnation of Christ as the ground of being
for their integration. In the final section, we will discuss the incarnational presence of
Christ in the therapeutic relationship between client and therapist. We will outline a
process of incarnational relational enactment through which the incarnate presence of
Christ facilitates true and healing renewal of the mind.
180 Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 2 of 13 --

1 3
Social theology and the kenotic community
Relational God
God’s triune nature is a defining feature of Christian theology. The Trinity simultaneously
identifies God as unified (homoousia) and differentiated (exousia), a single divinity rep-
resented in three persons. The most influential work in Western theology to deal with the
Trinity, Augustine’s De Trinitate, attempts to reconcile the inherent tension between God’s
singularity and multiplicity (Ables 2013). Augustine employed the use of metaphor to
illustrate the Trinity, describing the three persons as love, lover, and loved, with the defin-
ing feature being the relationship between persons (MacCulloch 2010). Father, Son, and
Spirit are constituted by virtue of the relational actions that link them. That is, Augustine
defined the Father and Son by their mutual love for one another and the Spirit as the pro-
cession of that love from one to the other. Augustine’s view of the Trinity is thus unified in
and through loving relationship (Ables 2013).
Subsequent readings of Augustine’s treatment of the Trinity have focused largely on
its relation to the 4 th
- and fifth century orthodoxy being codified in the language of the
Nicene and Apostles’ Creeds (MacCulloch 2010). Particular emphasis has been placed on
Augustine’s contributions to hierarchical understandings of the relationship between Trini-
tarian elements. As Johnson (1992) has noted, however, the ‘procession’ of relationships
described in Scripture is varied and nonhierarchical. In some passages, it is the Spirit pro-
ceeding forth from the Father, in some it is the Spirit giving life to the Son, and in still oth-
ers it is through the Son that we come to know the Father. Johnson (1992) suggested that
Augustine’s Trinity be understood primarily—by virtue of its relational language—as an
articulation of relationship characterized by mutual giving forth and receiving from. The
simultaneous and unified nature of Augustine’s Trinity also points to forms of relating that
do not involve dominance or hierarchy but are instead premised on mutuality.
Barth (1961) brought about a renewed interest in the theological significance of the
Trinity. Arguing for a strict focus on revelation as the primary means of knowing God,
Barth articulated his own analogy for the Trinity as revealer, revelation, and revealedness
(Torrance 2000). Barth unambiguously stated that God (i.e., creator), God’s act of revela-
tion (i.e., Spirit), and the content of that revelation (i.e., Christ) are all unified (1961). As
with Augustine, Barth’s analogy hinges upon the relational activity between elements of
the Trinity. His emphasis on revelation also inherently incorporates human subjectivity
into the relational nature of the Trinity. Not only is the Trinity defined by the simultaneous
relationality among its elements, but humanity is also entwined in that relationship as the
recipient of what is revealed. We are made to be simultaneous actors in the ongoing rela-
tional drama of the Trinity.
Barth’s theology points to revelation as the means through which humanity is drawn
into the relational nature of the divine. The embodied personhood of Christ is considered
the defining act of revelation and thus constitutive of God’s relational invitation to human-
ity (Barth 1961). Through the incarnation of Christ, the divine love shared among the
persons of the Trinity is extended to humanity. This love is also made vividly, tangibly
present among us through an act of fundamental humility. God’s self-emptying of divine
power and authority in order to take on the human form of Christ is referred to as kenosis
(Anderson 1979). For Barth, the kenosis is the vessel through which God’s relational love
is revealed among humankind. Through the kenosis—and thus the incarnation of Christ—
humanity is given a new model for relating, an invitation into reconciliation with God and
181	Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 3 of 13 --

1 3
with one another. We are called to enact the divine love by relating to one another as the
incarnated Christ related to us (Romans 14:5). Through the kenosis and incarnation, God
teaches us to meet one another as God has met us in Christ.
Kenotic community
Drawing on the kenosis as the defining feature of God’s relationship to humanity, the fel-
lowship summoned through incarnation can be understood as one of kenotic community
(Anderson 1979). The blueprint for this community is provided in and through the person-
hood of Christ. Its central precept is the self-emptying, empowering love of the kenosis.
That same love is repeatedly punctuated in the life and ministry of Christ, and the gospels
are replete with archetypal models for kenotic relating. There are the moments in which
Jesus subverts relational schema premised on dominance and subservience in order to
embrace (Matthew 8:3), invite (Matthew 9:9; Luke 5:27), engage (Luke 19:5–9), and for-
give (John 8:11). His rejection of hierarchy echoes the Trinitarian mutuality described by
Johnson (1992). Jesus also evokes the presence of this kenotic love as it is enacted among
humanity. In Luke 10:33–34, Jesus tells of the good Samaritan being so moved by love that
he set down his own preoccupations in order to care for the suffering of another. In Mat-
thew 25:35–40, Jesus affirms acts of hospitality, compassion, and humble relating from one
person to another as a fulfillment of the kenotic invitation. These examples all point to God
relating to the divine self through humanity. That is, acts of love derived from God reflect
back upon God and denote the divine nature (e.g., “God is love,” 1 John 4:8; Anderson
1979). The basic calling of the kenotic community is thus to encounter one another through
the self-humbling love made known to us in the revelation of Christ. By our participation
in the divine life, we become vessels for the transmission of God’s love between self and
others and between God and humanity.
In the kenosis, Christ divests himself of godliness in order to become human and relate
to humanity on human terms. In like manner, the kenotic community divests itself of
its human identification in order to participate in the very life of Christ. In doing so, the
kenotic community finds true purpose and meaning. Just as the loving relationship between
the figures of the Trinity is characterized by mutuality and parity (Johnson 1992), so too
are the relationships called forth by Christ in the kenotic community. Those in fellowship
must be willing to engage in a self-emptying with regard to the pretenses of dominance
and superiority. They must instead allow the spirit of Christ to penetrate their heart with
a yielding acceptance that then ensures that they will identify with the other on the same
level and footing.
Such humble openness aspires toward genuine relating between persons. It constitutes
what Buber (1958) has famously described as I-Thou relating, a level of deep openness to
the unfolding of relationship between individuals. Mismeeting, by contrast, includes some
level of insecurity, defensiveness, distancing, and claims premised on a perceived hierar-
chy. Mismeeting constitutes what Buber identified as I-It relating, a view of the other as
object rather than as mutually constituted subject.
Both Buber’s I-Thou relating and the genuine relating of kenotic community point to
the liminal space between mutually constituted subjects as the entry point through which
we encounter the divine. God, as the eternal Thou, is apprehended in and through relation-
ship, and the I of each individual is formed in the womblike in-between of I-Thou dialogue.
The dialogue allows each member to grow into the fullest embodiment of their own self
and to simultaneously embrace the fullest self of the other. This is made possible in and
182 Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 4 of 13 --

1 3
through an openness to the loving presence of Christ. It is the literal enactment of Jesus’
message from Matthew 18:20 that “where two or three are gathered in my name, there am
I in their midst” (NRSV). Entering into the genuine relating of the kenotic community thus
becomes a vivid experience through which we encounter the incarnate presence of Christ
and, through Christ’s spirit, participate in the divine life.
Interpersonal psychology
Interpersonal neurobiology
Human development is a complex, recursive system of interrelationships among the bio-
logical, psychological, and social aspects of personhood. We are formed, nurtured, and
socialized in and through relationship. Psychology has made significant strides in the past
20 years toward formally recognizing the significance of relationship in the formation of
personality and development of brain neurology (Schore 2011). The interdisciplinary field
of interpersonal neurobiology has emerged to account for the role of caregiver relation-
ships in childhood brain development (Gambrel et al. 2016). The core premise of interper-
sonal neurobiology is that healthy relationships with caregivers in infancy and early child-
hood allow the brain to develop optimal neural connections between the limbic system and
the prefrontal cortex (Fishbane 2007). These neural connections are then thought to facil-
itate adaptive psychological processes such as emotion regulation, acceptance, empathy,
and coping (Siegel 2007). Relationships with early caregivers (i.e., attachment) are central
to this process as they represent the initial interpersonal milieu through which infants and
children learn to relate to themselves and their environment (Siegel 2007; Vaughn et al.
2008).
Much of early brain activity occurs in the right orbitofrontal cortex (OFC), the region
of the brain most central to attachment and the attachment relationship (Minagawa-Kawai
et al. 2009; Parsons et al. 2013). The OFC serves the vital integrating function of coor-
dinating social communication, empathic attunement, emotional regulation, registration
of body states, stimulus appraisal, and self-awareness (Siegel and Hartzell 2003). One’s
genetic blueprint erects a rough neural scaffold of micro and macro anatomy connecting
the OFC with the more primal limbic areas such as the amygdala and thalamus (Fishbane
2007). These limbic structures are key for the processing of adverse emotions, particularly
fear and distress (LeDoux 2002). Early relational experiences fine-tune these neural con-
nections between the OFC and the limbic system into the specific neural pathways that
help to shape a person’s emotional identity (LeDoux 1998). It is generally believed that
relational experiences of acceptance, nurturance, and affection in infancy and early child-
hood substantially aid in the normative development of these neural connections. Such
theoretical assertions are supported by imaging studies associating supportive and attentive
parenting with more developed neural circuitry for emotion regulation and stress manage-
ment (Luby et al. 2012). Relationships thus represent the “ongoing experience [that] gradu-
ally transforms a child’s neural configuration... one synapse at a time” (Lewis et al. 2000,
p. 151).
Relationships continue to play an important role in brain development throughout the
lifespan. The presence of supportive caregiver relationships in childhood has been shown
to predict emotional stability and self-acceptance in adolescence as well as the qual-
ity of romantic relationships in adulthood (Gambrel et al. 2016). During the transition to
183	Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 5 of 13 --

1 3
parenthood, changes in brain anatomy take place as parents prepare for and then form new
relationships with the infant and child (Kim et al. 2010; Lambert 2012). Long-term friend-
ships and romantic relationships have also been associated with both brain structure and
function in late adulthood (Acevedo et al. 2012). These examples serve as a testament to
the importance of interpersonal connections over time in shaping normal psychological
functioning.
Psychopathology as broken relationship
When viewed from the perspective of interpersonal psychology, psychopathology repre-
sents a rupture in the relationships that help constitute healthy functioning (Thoburn and
Sexton 2016). Given the importance of secure relationships in cultivating emotion regula-
tion, empathy, and self-awareness, ruptures in our core relationships are thought to have a
detrimental effect on normal functioning (Slipp 1984). All relationships can have an impact
on psychological functioning, but close and emotionally intimate relationships are particu-
larly important. So too is the relationship that we have with ourselves—comprised of the
myriad of thoughts, beliefs, and feelings that we have about the self. When either form of
core relationship—either our relationship with the self or our relationship to those with
whom we have been emotionally vulnerable—are ruptured, there is a resulting dysregula-
tion of our psychological systems (Siegel 2007; Slipp 1984).
This has been most clearly demonstrated through the research on various forms of inse-
cure attachment. Disruptions in the infant-caregiver relationship as a result of neglect,
inconsistency, or hostility can lead to poor emotion regulation capacities on the part of the
infant (Zimmer-Gembeck et al. 2017). From a cognitive perspective, these insecure attach-
ment relationships can also lead to the development of foundational schemas for viewing
the self as damaged or inadequate and other people as dangerous or untrustworthy (Simard
et al. 2011). Maladaptive schemas formed through disrupted attachment relationships in
infancy can have a subsequently detrimental effect on other interpersonal relationships later
in life (McLean et al. 2014). The brain is hardwired for survival, and these maladaptive
schemas frequently employ an underlying fear response that is mediated by the limbic sys-
tem (Fellous and LeDoux 2005). This conditioned fear response results in aversion to the
healthy forms of emotional intimacy that characterize adaptive interpersonal functioning.
One of the hallmark features of a psychiatric disorder as defined by the most recent
diagnostic manual is impairment in one’s interpersonal relationships (American Psychiat-
ric Association 2013). While a simplistic causal attribution might view such impairment
as resulting from the disorder itself, a wealth of contemporary research points to the bidi-
rectional relationship between intrapersonal psychological distress and impaired interper-
sonal relationships (Whisman and Beach 2013; Whisman and Uebelacker 2003). Discord
in interpersonal relationships can cause, trigger, or compound psychological distress. That
same psychological stress can also make it difficult for individuals to fully engage in the
behaviors that constitute a healthy interpersonal relationship.
Limbic revision
Just as relationships can be a source of psychological distress, so too can they be a source
of much-needed healing. At a neurological level, the healing that occurs within relation-
ship likely unfolds through a process that again involves the linkages between the limbic
and the prefrontal cortex structures of the brain. This neurological change occurs when
184 Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 6 of 13 --

1 3
novel experiences are strong enough to aid in the formation of new neurons and synaptic
pathways between these structures. The limbic system is thought to be of primary impor-
tance in the processing of emotions, particularly the fear response that characterizes core
maladaptive schemas (LeDoux 1998). Repeated exposure to emotionally salient social
interactions in the absence of an aversive stimulus allows for the deconditioning of that
same fear response (Hayes et al. 2008). This process is likely aided by the genesis of new
neurons in the hippocampus region of the limbic system (i.e., neurogenesis; Apple et al.
2017), the strengthening of synaptic connections associated with adaptive emotion regula-
tion (i.e. synaptogenesis; Aupperle et al. 2013; Cisler et al. 2014, 2016), and the deteriora-
tion of synapses involved in dysregulation (Li et al. 2009). Therapeutic change may thus
rely on the complex processes of neurogenesis and synaptogenesis that occur in the context
of relationship.
These findings lend support to the Lewis et al. (2000) view that one person can aid
in restructuring the limbic brain of another, which they call limbic revision. This claim
can be summarized by their axiom that neural patterns established through interpersonal
relationship can only be revised through the milieu of interpersonal relating. The work of
psychotherapy is, at its core, a process of interpersonal limbic revision. Buber suggested
that learning and formation occur in the in-between of persons, that is, where persons inter-
sect through dialogue in all its forms (Kramer and Gawlick 2003). An I-It relationship is
characterized by one’s view of the other as object and as fixed in time and there to be acted
upon. To use the language of psychology, it is to see the other only through the lens of
our own preexisting cognitive schemas and the expectation that patterns experienced in
the past will be fulfilled in the present. Buber’s characterization of the I-Thou relation-
ship, in contrast, is premised on mutual subjectivity in which both individuals are dynamic
actors that bring life to the dialogue between them. I-Thou relating creates a fertile holding
place in which the processes of learning and formation take place. The actors move beyond
expectations based on preestablished schemas and instead see one another in the fullness of
their mutual humanity.
This space between I and thou is the fomenting source of limbic revision and healing.
When individuals experience one another as dynamic actors, fully embracing the human-
ness of the other, they are freed to relate in new ways. They are no longer tethered to previ-
ous dysfunctional patterns or schemata; instead, they are able to respond to one another in
each moment with genuine love and affirmation. This gives rise to a series of punctuated,
emotionally salient interactions that form the basis for limbic revision. These corrective
emotional experiences occur when thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that previously per-
petuated broken relationships are instead responded to with thoughts, feelings, and behav-
iors rooted in love (Teyber and McClure 2011). Over time, experiential stimuli previously
associated with fear or hurt—and thus a need for corresponding self-protection—become
neurologically linked with greater self-regulation (Tabibnia and Radecki 2018). Most cen-
tral to the development of healthy interpersonal relating is the process through which indi-
viduals become increasingly comfortable with the vulnerability necessary for emotional
intimacy (Lewis et al. 2000). Through the corrective emotional experiences of genuine
I-Thou relating, they learn that vulnerability and intimacy, rather than hurt and harm, are
conducive to deeper relationship.
Research on psychotherapy outcomes indicates that it is the therapeutic relationship,
much more so than the therapeutic approach or formalized techniques, that gives rise to the
change associated with corrective emotional experiences (Norcross 2011). Limbic revision
is thus premised on genuine relating rather than the leveraging of specific data or a particu-
lar psychological theory.
185	Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 7 of 13 --

1 3
Integration and the incarnational relational enactment
Efforts to integrate psychology and theology frequently focus on the layering of paral-
lel data. Our review of social theology and interpersonal psychology in this paper sug-
gests that relationship, rather than data, lies at the core of healing. The essence of what
it is to be Christian, therefore, is to take part in the kenotic community by embrac-
ing the relational presence of Christ. In like manner, the therapeutic relationship is one
manifestation of the kenotic community. Participation in the kenotic community of the
client-therapist relationship and the I-Thou relating that facilitates corrective emotional
experience are both contingent upon a humbling, genuine relationship without pretense
of power or hierarchical authority. From a Christian standpoint, this form of relating can
be understood most simply as an enactment of love made possible through the presence
of Christ.
Recognizing the relational presence of Christ at the core of both psychological and
theological healing allows for the use of a new term to describe the corrective emotional
experience. These therapeutic encounters only unfold within the real presence of Christ,
and the real presence of Christ can occur only within the context of human encounters
(Anderson 1990, pp. 186–187). We refer to these punctuated moments of heightened
emotional experiencing, fomented in and through the presence of Christ, as incarna-
tional relational enactment. Drawing together both social theology and interpersonal
psychology, we describe incarnational relational enactment as immediate, personal,
premised upon unconditional acceptance, and dialectical.
Immediacy refers to meeting in the here-and-now moment, a meeting that appre-
hends the full potential for transformation in each moment. Immediacy is the gateway to
attunement, critical moments in therapy when the therapist is completely in sync emo-
tionally, mentally, and spiritually with the client. It is also contingent upon the theologi-
cal assertion that revelation is not chronologically fixed but instead occurs in each new
moment through the spirit of Christ. This is the relational nature of revelation described
by Barth (1961). Through the resurrection, the incarnational presence of Christ tran-
scends the temporal specificity of 1 st -century Palestine (Anderson 1979). Christ is pre-
sent in the immediacy of each new moment of history. The spirit of Christ is thus pre-
sent in each new moment of therapy, perpetually inviting both therapist and client into
greater attunement with one another.
This attuned relating is also highly personal. Therapist and client are immersed in the
presence of Christ, who knows each individual down to the numbering of the hairs on their
head (Matthew 10:30). The relationship thus takes the form of an encounter in which both
participants seek to know and be known by the other. To be truly known by another is to
be seen not as a collection of expected behaviors or prior schemas but as a free and ful-
some individual. This is what Barth (2003) describes as “man’s free confrontation with
his fellow man” (p. 7) and what Tillich (1952) identifies as the “courage to affirm the self”
through encounter with another (p. 91). Understanding the unique personhood that con-
stitutes the other is necessary for accurate empathy and affirmation (Miller and Rollnick
2013) and client response specificity (Teyber and McClure 2011), all of which are instru-
mental in a corrective emotional experience. Knowing and being known is ultimately vital
to a healed existence because, as Anderson (1979) notes, “It offers each person... an actual
growth into the reality of his own personhood, which is the capacity to live in love” (p.
313). It is the reestablishment of a positive movement of being in relation to an other that
makes therapy therapeutic (Lee and Balswick 1989, p. 306).
186 Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 8 of 13 --

1 3
Incarnational relational enactment also moves beyond knowing into a state of uncon-
ditional acceptance. The very essence of incarnation is God’s continual embrace of the
flawed human condition. In therapy, this takes the form of each participant being received
into relationship by virtue of their common humanity with Christ, without any requisite
qualifying characteristics or actions (Anderson 1979). Incarnational relational enactment
allows the client to experience affective states that they have traditionally repressed because
of fear of punishment, rejection, abandonment, or being used. The great therapeutic act is
in allowing the client the paradox of feeling and expressing both love and hate toward the
therapist—to initiate feelings of a potentially killing rage, knowing that the therapist will
survive (Winnicott 1988). Again, this is not the therapist simply acting like Jesus; rather, it
is a yielding to and participation in the healing movement of Christ. The incarnational rela-
tional enactment thus allows the client to express the fullness of their personhood without
fear of rejection or dismissal, to experience loving relationship without qualifications or
conditions.
The unqualified love of Christ is held in perpetual tension with the simultaneous call to
repentance. God both accepts and reforms. These distinctive theological theses form two
poles of the dialectic in which we encounter the spirit of Christ. Growth and transforma-
tion, often described as the process of becoming, constitute the synthesis of these poles.
Through the incarnational relational enactment, this dialectic characterizes the therapeutic
process. The client is accepted as they are, free to enact their personhood without fear of
rejection. At the same time, the therapist draws on the intimacy of I-Thou relating to explic-
itly identify maladaptive patterns and formulate new, more adaptive responses (Teyber and
McClure 2011). When synthesized, this simultaneous acceptance and change allows for
therapeutic growth.
In the original Greek of the New Testament, such a transformation of the mind was
described as metanoia. Often written in modern English as repentance, the word more lit-
erally translates as a turning (meta) of the mind (noia). This metanoia is the telos of the
incarnational relational enactment, its ultimate purpose. Metanoia is the summation of the
neurogenesis and synaptogenesis that underlie limbic revision, which in turn changes the
effortful process of learning new ways of interacting with the self and with others into an
effortless process of being in relationship with others. It is a renewal of personhood, the
capacity to live in love (Anderson 1979), and a willingness to relate to another—and ulti-
mately to Christ—with intimacy and vulnerability.
Conclusion
Psychological and biblical truths provide context for integration, but the content of inte-
gration is the ground of being, Christ himself. Through Christ, the relational nature of the
divine is extended to humankind. The love extended by God through Christ is grounded in
self-emptying kenosis, and humankind is invited to live into this love through participation
in the kenotic community. The therapeutic relationship constitutes one manifestation of the
kenotic community, where Christ is made manifest in the midst of the caring relationship.
It is through the therapeutic dialogue that both client and therapist encounter God’s life-
altering love and healing.
The interpersonal process between therapist and client is optimally suited for attaining
therapeutic change because it leverages the relational nature of human neurology and psy-
chology. Both adaptive and maladaptive human development are formed in relationship.
187	Pastoral Psychology (2021) 70:179–190

-- 9 of 13 --

1 3
Therapeutic healing is thus premised on an improved capacity for relational functioning.
The client must undergo iterative corrective emotional experiences that allow for a pro-
cess of limbic revision. Experiential stimuli previously associated with fear and hurt are,
through genuine I-Thou relating, gradually paired with experiences of acceptance and self-
regulation. New neurons and synaptic connections are formed that ultimately sustain this
more adaptive relational framework.
This process of therapeutic change is made possible in and through the presence of
Christ and the manifestation of kenotic community. The presence of Christ in the midst of
therapy gives rise to moments of incarnational relational enactment. Each participant in the
therapeutic dialogue enters a state of heightened connectedness to self, other, and Christ. In
this heightened state of connecting, there is a focus on the immediate, here-and-now nature
of God’s relational revelation in each new moment. The attunement is also deeply personal,
allowing the client to grow into the fullness of their personhood. It is premised on uncon-
ditional, unqualified acceptance of the other and yet simultaneously calls for a process of
growth and change. The resulting dialectic is synthesized through limbic revision, resulting
in a literal renewing of the mind and leading to a new capacity to live in love.

FROM  ATTACHMENT  TO  DISORDERS
🔍 How Personality Disorders Can Emerge
Not everyone with attachment trauma develops a personality disorder. Genetics, physical issues, temperament, support systems, and resilience all play a role. 
When attachment wounds are deep and persistent, they can shape the way someone copes with emotions and relationships.
Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD):
Rooted in fear of abandonment
Emotional instability, intense relationships, identity confusion
Often linked to caregivers who were emotionally unpredictable or invalidating
Narcissistic Personality Disorder (NPD):
Rooted in fear of vulnerability and inadequacy
Grandiosity, need for admiration, lack of empathy
Often linked to caregivers who were critical, conditional, or emotionally unavailable
These disorders aren’t character flaws—they’re adaptations to pain. They reflect unmet needs for safety, validation, and connection.

Thinking Family from the Trinity
Love, by definition, is directed toward another. Therefore, love cannot exist where there is not a plurality of persons.” — Richard of St. Victor  (Persons in Relation)
“Many contemporary theologians see the community of the Trinity as a model for God’s community of the church. In God, one finds mutual love between persons-in-relationship who recognize the equality and also value the differences of ‘the others.’ Although human relationships can never reach the unity of being one finds in God, they can be a dim analogy.” (Trinity in Relation)—Robin Parry
“Christian theology grounds family systems categories in the very activity of God in the world.” —Craig Nessan (Theology as systemic context for the world)

title: Slides: Psalm 13
content:
-1—
0—
+1—
Psalm 13
Abandonment and Adoration
“How long, Lor d? W ill you forget me fore ve r?
How long w ill you hide your face f rom me?”
(Psalm 13:1 NIV)
Surveying the Garden
This psalm pursues a deep quest. It grapples with our sense of God’s absence,
which could raise a question of God’s abandonment of us or our inattentive-
ness to him.
This psalm’s questioning is not just about knowing God, but about find-
ing a restored sense of God’s presence. That is the quest for intimacy in the
Psalms. Silence can easily be misread, but he is there.
These passages resonate with people like Job, the author of Ecclesiastes,
and the countless pilgrims, like you, who want to know God as present and
living. The shared experience of humanity wrestles with a sense of absence,
especially in the midst of pain and doubt.
With the void comes distress, anxiety, depression, loneliness, and some-
times deep despair or agony. Today, the garden echoes with emptiness and
prayers of urgency. The deep yearning for intimacy permeates every fiber of
our being. This hunger cuts to the core of our existence as persons.
The whole of our being is at stake in this psalm. We are facing who we are
and will become in life’s challenging pilgrimage. Our body will face the
threat of aging and eventually death. Our emotions are often spent in sor-
row or weariness. Our thinking is often interrupted, consumed by questions
that entomb us.
4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 100	4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 100 	18/12/25 8:48 PM	18/12/25 8:48 PM

-- 1 of 8 --

Psalm 13 • 101
—-1
—0
—+1
Who are you in “moments of missing” like a “minus one,” in a romantic
setting, feeling all alone? You cannot be the same “you” when a beloved
other is absent. You want to go through life as a “plus one.” Even those who
are single need family and friends to maintain emotional health. You need
others in order to fully be you, especially as your anchor in the storm. Hope
remains but diminishes as you are worn down.
Abandonment issues often arise from unfulfi lled expectations. These
may occur due to prolonged absences or becoming aware that the care of
another is lacking. What is missing is feeling valued. This may manifest as
feeling forgotten, perhaps devalued, or missing the assurance that you
matter. It sucks the life out of you. Being valued and belonging give us the
gift of being found desirable. Our life has meaning. That is what God’s love
establishes for you.
This void echoes with your absence, a longing for reunion.
This psalm reveals that even in life’s most confusing moments, we find
God’s unwavering faithfulness. He knows you and says, “They are singing our
song,” and you know you have found the One who resonates with your soul.
Seated at his table, with bread and wine of remembering, you recall this
One as a faithful friend. He is not lost in the past; he has always been there
for you. He is present as he remembers you to himself. Presence comes in the
remembering of feeling at ease and at home.
Upon returning to his table and eating with him, you recognize that his
absence reminded you that you desire him, and he is fond of you returning
regularly. Absence was anticipation.
The sun comes out. You feel like celebrating. You now experience the hap-
piness that overcomes feelings of neglect caused by your misplaced focus.
Jesus’s presence brings an attitude adjustment. He has been patient,
waiting for your return. He dances over the void and invites you to join him.
Jesus says, “I have you. I always do. Breathe in My readiness to stand by you,
walk with you, and carry you through. Then sing out that song of gladness. Let
our song expand in you with all the passion and joy that can be exhaled from you.
Let My Spirit renew you, wash your pain, and bring you into the pleasure of Our
company. You are not alone.”
Into the Garden with God
The psalmist acknowledges but is also concerned by our profound longing
for connection. Th is longing is not a bad thing. It echoes a spoken love,
shared in the past, still yearning for reunion.
4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 101	4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 101 	18/12/25 8:48 PM	18/12/25 8:48 PM

-- 2 of 8 --

102 • The Psalms
-1—
0—
+1—
Yearning for the face of the one you love reveals your heart’s orienta-
tion. When they are gone, your face loses its glow, and the light in your eyes
dims.
This psalm illuminates our core commitment, allowing us to feel our
pulse rise as we seek to find this kind of love that hungers for face-to-face
connection.
This desire to see the face of God is portrayed in the Aaronic blessing:
The Lord bless you, and keep you;
25 the Lord cause His face to shine on you, and be gracious to you;
26 the Lord lift up His face to you, and give you peace.
(Numbers 6:24–26 NASB)
This is a prayer with a goal of a transforming gaze. When the Lord turns
toward us, we bask in the warmth of his shining face. This personal connec-
tion, initiated by Jesus and reflected in our faces, continues to have an im-
pact until his grace finally reaches our hearts.
In 1 Corinthians, being face-to-face with God is anticipated as our ul-
timate goal. For now, God is mediated in a reflected way, like seeing some-
one standing behind you glimpsed in a mirror. In the mirror, you may
catch an echoed image of them; you have to turn around to look at them
face-to-face.
This reflection of God points us to real ity. It awakens a desire for deep
togetherness. Paul says,
For now we see only a reflection as in a mirror;
then we shall see face to face. Now I know in part;
then I shall know fully, even as I am fully known.
(1 Corinthians 13:12 NIV)
Knowing and being known is the final bliss.
Today, we are like-minded with Paul, intent on the time when face-to-
face encounters achieve the fullness of love and joy. We live with the current
reality and acknowledge the emerging sense of distance.
Jesus sees you on the other side of the maze of life. He promises, “I am here. Talk
to me. Eat my Meal. Be with those who love Me. My life is the shared life; share it
with Me.”
4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 102	4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 102 	18/12/25 8:48 PM	18/12/25 8:48 PM

-- 3 of 8 --

Psalm 13 • 103
—-1
—0
—+1
Visions of Paradise
A maze is full of anticipation and waiting to reach the far end. How long one will
be in the maze depends on how well one knows the way, or desires to get to some-
one waiting at the other end. It is full of mystery, but the anxiety of the journey
is worth it for the reconnection at the finish.
Along the Garden Path
“How much longer?” is a typical back-seat complaint. A child’s anxiety re-
veals that they have not yet learned to enjoy the journey. But it is an honest
question that needs an answer.
Telling a story about the joy of seeing the person at the destination can
refocus on hope, creating a picture of being met with a smile, a hug, and a
good laugh.
Th is path we are on today is not easy. It is a long pilgrimage. It begins
with being perplexed.
The psalmist is stuck between memories of connection and delight with his
God, as well as the impending, threatening possibility of death. Enemies lurk
near the psalmist; they will be glad when he is gone; their jeers are in his ears.
Th is is a grief observed, life out of control. Ahead is the slog of daily
endurance.
4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 103	4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 103 	18/12/25 8:48 PM	18/12/25 8:48 PM

-- 4 of 8 --

104 • The Psalms
-1—
0—
+1—
Four times the words echo out on the “Welcome Mat” of this psalm:
“How long?” There is a tense feeling that, if this is a game of hide-and-seek,
it has been too long! This is an intensely personal path. Fear floods one’s
thoughts. Is this rejection? Is this absence or simply neglect? Whatever it is,
it tears at the heart. How long?
His thoughts, a tangled maze, present a bewildering series of consider-
ations. Reflecting on these feelings is an isolating experience. Plans and
possibilities that were a cause for joy have now become a racket inside our
heads.
During the storm, you are covered with a blanket of grief. You wish you
could just hide, to be left alone. It is made worse by a further hailstorm of
agitators that batter and ridicule you.
Finally, your anxiety begs for intervention. You want to be heard. You
need answers. You want to be seen.
The darkness of death dissolves as you awaken to heaven’s breaking
light. Now, there is a glow in your eye, not coming from within; it reflects his
light. The Spirit has found you and brought you to the welcoming presence
of God. You feel courageous.
Then, an awareness of God’s unfailing love comes to you. The rain and
sunshine of emotions all come at once. The cleansing rays of hope create a
rainbow, a reminder of God’s covenant love. Suddenly, you are sustained by
trust. Your heart enlarges. The storm clouds are swept away.
Then comes the song. The melody is simple and sweet, sung from long
ago. It is the melody of divine hope.
You realize that the feeling of unrequited love was simply a result of your
forgetfulness. God’s love comes to us first and is unconditional. That love
never fails. What remains is our response: to be free, made whole by the One
who loved us all along. Belonging is the final resolve.
This is the spring that we drink from; this is the source of our exuber-
ance. The song inside breaks into expressions of simple desire, to bask in the
generous goodness of this One who is our resounding joy.
Much of our life is anticipation. But stay on the path and feel the sacred touch
of unreserved grace and healing at the end. Take the step, knowing it is an ad-
venture together.
4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 104	4046-145940_MartyFolsom_3P.indd 104 	18/12/25 8:48 PM	18/12/25 8:48 PM

Your daily gift s

Taking Reflections with You
You have probably known dark places of disappointment. Today you have an
appointment to meet with the One who is always available—the appoint-
ment is for us to take time, not because he is hard to get in to see!
• Do any words in this psalm resonate with your sense of God
not being available for you? What words awaken in you the
enduring faithfulness of God?
• How did this psalm awaken your desire to cry out, “How
long?” In what way did the movement that began with des-
peration and ends in exaltation help you to see the nature of
God in your life in a new way?
• How did this psalm center you regarding what is missing and
what your ultimate joy is in fulfi lling who you are? What is
your identity in relation to suffering? Do you feel a victim in
any way?
• What does this psalm reveal about your relationship with God?
To whom or what do you give your whole heart and being?
What does it feel like to awaken to One who affirms his never-
ending love?
• When his love breaks through in this psalm, what do you
hear spoken to you?
• What words of response would you mirror back to God from
this psalm?

Fallen humanity is engaged in the fateful turn to self-sufficiency. 
Humanity usurps from God the role of judge of wisdom and truth. 
As a result, there is blaming, shaming, and a propensity to do what is right in our own eyes. 
The capacity for connecting with God is there, but the individual will struggle for independence and reject God as Father and Jesus as Lord. 
The fear of losing control of one’s self-determination and self-fulfillment, which are illusions, begins a subtle process of developing defense mechanisms that create a self-made prison. 
From this lonely place, one pursues all the vices to nurse the loss, the emptiness, and the pain.

But we all, with unveiled face, beholding as in a mirror the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from glory to glory, just as from the Lord, the Spirit. 
The reflection unveiled - able to receive 
Transformation - changed into His likeness
This is the work of the Spirit
RECREATED IN CHRIST
To be “in Christ” is not a location; it is a relation OF HEALTH – IN LOVE
To be loved so we may love 
To be “outside Christ” would be to resist his love and grace. HUMAN DISORDER 
All forms of ignoring, rejecting, neglecting, self-willing, and evading cannot stop the love of Christ for us, but they do affect our experience of the relationship. 
YOU ARE MY BELOVED CHILD	As My Son has shown you	As our love enfolds you	As My Spirit speaks in youABBA, I BELONG TO YOU	As you have made me your child	As you accompany me	As you send me and go with me

title: Slides: Vocational Self-Narrative Instructions and Rubric
content:
Theology and Counseling
COUN 5773
Appendix A
Instructions for Vocational Spiritual Self-Narrative
10% of course grade
Who am I? Who am I in the present, and who have I been? Who am I in my social relations? Who am I in my vocational calling? Who am I in relationship to God (or my conception of ultimate reality)? Who am I as a person in my CMHC program as a next step in the journey of my calling in life? 
This assignment is completed through a written narrative, thus the task is to complete:
A 1000-1500 word narrative essay that a) is carefully composed with no or almost no writing errors; b) provides response to the above questions, though the structure of how this is done may vary; c) demonstrates self-understanding and respect for others; d) responds to some of the spiritual, theological, and philosophical notions found in Smith and other assigned course readings, along with other valuable sources at the writer’s discretion; and e) APA-formatted citations as necessary. 
This assignment addresses Course Learning Outcome 1: Compose a self-narrative which identifies religious, spiritual, and ethical self-understanding in relation to the vocation of mental health counseling (CACREP Standard 2b, 2d, and 2g).
Grading Rubric: 
Grading Area
A-range 
B-range 
C-range 
D- and F-range 
Followed Directions (10%) 
All directions are properly followed. 
Almost all directions are properly followed. 
Several lapses in following directions. 
Numerous lapses in following directions. 
Quality of Written Composition (10%) 
No or almost no errors in writing. Quality of writing is altogether appropriate to a course at the master’s level. 
Several writing errors or problems. Quality of writing is mostly appropriate to a course at the master’s level. 
A number of writing errors or problems in writing. Quality of writing includes lapses inappropriate to a course at the master’s level. 
Regular errors or problems in writing. Quality of writing has regular lapses inappropriate to a course at the master’s level.
 
APA Formatting (10%) 
Documentation is entirely or almost entirely in conformity with APA formatting and style. 
Documentation is mostly in conformity with APA formatting and style. 
Documentation has regular lapses in conformity with APA formatting and style. 
Documentation lacks conformity with APA formatting and style. 
Self-Narration and Storytelling 
(40%) 
Substantially narrates personal narrative in a manner that provides efficient and appropriate sharing of personal identity, relations, and vocation. 
Mostly provides substantial personal narrative in a manner that provides efficient and appropriate sharing of personal identity, relations, and vocation. 
Partially provides substantial personal narrative in a manner that provides efficient and appropriate sharing of personal identity, relations, and vocation. 
Provide little to no substantial personal narrative in a manner that provides efficient and appropriate sharing of personal identity, relations, and vocation. 
Self-Interpretation 
(30%) 
Constructs a mature self-interpretation of one’s personal identity and vocational understanding, even if in process. 
Constructs a mostly developed self-interpretation of one’s personal identity and vocational understanding, even if in process. 
Constructs a partially developed self-interpretation of one’s personal identity and vocational understanding, even if in process. 
Constructs a minimally developed self-interpretation of one’s personal identity and vocational understanding, even if in process.

title: syllabus COUN 5773 Theology and Counseling - 2026 (Part 1/2)
content:
python-docx not available
College of Social and Behavioral Sciences Master of Arts in Clinical Mental Health Counseling COUN 5773 Theology and Counseling Spring 2026 nstructor : Marty Folsom, Ph.D. NU E-mail: Marty.Folsom@northwestu.edu Personal E-mail: drtrinity@comcast.net Phone: 425-3434 - 494 Office Hours: By appointment only, in-person , Zoom, or Messenger Video Chat Class Schedule: January 6 (Tu), January 20 (Tu), February 3 (Tu), February 17 (Tu) , March 3 (Tu), March 17 (Tu), March 31 (Tu), April 14 ( Tu ), 4 hours weekly Required Text: Anderson, Ray S. (2010), Christians who Counsel: The Vocation of Wholistic Therapy . Wipf & Stock. Callaway, Kutter, and Whitney, William B. (2022). Theology for Psychology and Counseling: An Invitation to Holistic Christian Practice . Baker Academic. . Course Description: This course presents a Christian theological perspective and encourages the student to think deeply about their own beliefs about the nature of humans in light of contemporary issues in the field of counseling. Course Objectives: The student will: Compose a self-narrative that identifies religious, spiritual, and ethical self-understanding in relation to the vocation of mental health counseling (CACREP Standard 2b, 2d, and 2g). Assess anthropological assumptions in religious and philosophical texts (CACREP Standard 2a, 2b, 2d, 2g, and 2h). Summarize several written communications on the integration of faith and mental health counseling for use by peers (CACREP Standard 2d and 2g). Construct an integrative research paper that develops faith and philosophical sources in an integration with counseling ethics, justice, and advocacy (CACREP Standard 2b, 2d, 2g, and 2g). CACREP Standards: 2F.2.a. multicultural and pluralistic characteristics within and among diverse groups nationally and internationally 2F.2.b. theories and models of multicultural counseling, cultural identity development, and social justice and advocacy 2F.2.d. the impact of heritage, attitudes, beliefs, understandings, and acculturative experiences on an individual ' s views of others 2F.2.g. the impact of spiritual beliefs on clients ' and counselors ' worldviews 2F. 2.h. strategies for identifying and eliminating barriers, prejudices, and processes of intentional and unintentional oppression and discrimination Outcomes Measurement Objectives Self Narrative Assigned Readings Discussion Boards R eading / Article Review Integration Paper Professionalism and Participation Field Work: Anthropological Observation 2F.2.a (A) X X 2F.2.b (B) X X X 2F.2.d (D) X X X X X X X 2F.2.g (G) X X X X X 2F.2.h (H) X X X Academic Honesty: Northwest University expects honesty from students in all areas, including their academic lives. Academic dishonesty is a serious violation of University standards and may result in the students ' receiving an " F " on the assignment, receiving an " F " for the course, or possibly being dismissed from the University. Instances of academic dishonesty are coordinated through the respective graduate program office and typically reported to the Provost ' s Office. Academic dishonesty includes cheating on assignments or examinations, submitting the same (or essentially the same) paper in more than one course without prior consent of the current assigning professor(s), sabotaging another student ' s work, and plagiarizing. Plagiarism is " using someone else ' s ideas or phrasing and representing those ideas or phrasing as our own, either on purpose or through carelessness " (MLA). Any student disciplined by a faculty member for alleged academic dishonesty has the right to appeal the disciplinary action. The student should initiate his/her appeal according to procedures outlined in the Northwest University Student Academic Appeals Policy, which is available in the Provost Office. Attendance Policy: Class attendance is extremely important for student learning and achievement of specified course outcomes and, in turn, the program aims. Hence, attendance at all class sessions is expected. At minimum, students absent for a class session will need to make up any missed assignments or exercises and complete the expected readings. A grade penalty will be applied if more than one class session is missed. Additional policies and consequences are at the discretion of the individual professor of the course. More than one missed class session will result in failure of the course and require a retake to continue in the program. In this circumstance, the student is encouraged to withdraw from the course and retake the class when it is offered again. In the event of unusual circumstances (e.g., extended illness, emergency), a student may appeal the automatic course failure. A proposal outlining a plan for satisfactory course completion will be required, composed by the professor and agreed to by the student. In addition, the plan will require approval by the program director and Dean. Disability Accommodations and Procedures: For students with learning differences and/or physical disabilities, as defined by the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, Northwest University takes an individual and holistic approach to providing accommodations . Accommodations are developed and approved on an individual basis by the Student Development Office, not the CMHC program or individual faculty members. Students needing accommodations for a diagnosed disability should follow the procedures provided on the Student Development Office's website for disability accommodations. Communication Policy: Timely communication between the professor and students is important to the overall functioning of the program. Therefore, both students and faculty should respond to email or phone messages within two business days. If the professor is out-of-office, he or she should have an automatic reply message (on email) or voicemail recording (phone) providing the date of his/her return, as well as an in-office contact for urgent requests. Grading Policy: Grading is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to monitor their progress accordingly. Assignments and tests will generally be graded within two weeks. Final papers and projects may take up to three weeks. The professor will let you know if there are exceptional circumstances preventing a timely return of grades. If you have concerns about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator, or program Chair. Late Work Policy: It is the student ' s responsibility to meet all deadlines for class assignments. Grades for each late assignment submitted may be penalized by 20%. It is up to the instructor whether they will accept late work. Mechanics of the Course: A) Class Sessions Class sessions will be used for a variety of purposes , including: Lecture Class and Group Discussion Writing B) Response, Research, or Other Written Assignments Discussion Boards (20%) The weekly discussion boards, guided by a question prompt, require a 250-plus word evaluative response from the student. Students will be graded according to their response and will not be required to respond to others. Feedback should be constructive and on topic only, even and especially when challenging the ideas of others. However, the main purpose of the discussion board is for students to provide an evaluative response to a topic to develop evaluations of topics in our subject matter and development in quality of written communication. ( Due on the Sunday following class by midnight ) Vocational-Spiritual Self-Narrative (10% of course grade): Who am I? Who am I in the present, and who have I been? Who am I in my social relations? Who am I in my vocational calling? Who am I in relationship to God (or my conception of ultimate reality)? Who am I as a person studying mental health counseling as a next step in the journey of my calling in life? Written communication: A 1000-1500 word narrative essay that a) is carefully composed with no or almost no writing errors; b) provides response to the above questions, though the structure of how may vary; c) demonstrates self-understanding and respect for others; d) responds to some of the spiritual, theological, and philosophical notions found in Smith and other assigned course readings, along with other valuable sources at the writer ' s discretion; and e) APA-formatted citations as necessary. (Due on February 22) Reading/Article Review (20%) Construct a 1000-1500-word response to the constructive ideas found in an integrative reading or article of approximately 20-40 pages of scholarly material that engages the intersection of theology and counseling. Students may choose from the options below or propose another option to the professor in the form of an e-mail request with explanation as to why the proposed article is a good candidate for review. (Due on March 8 ) Several representative and preapproved options (posted as PDFs in Discovery) include: Augustine, D. (2019). " Equality, Justice, and Peac

Faith and Counseling Integration Paper (40%) A 2000-3000-word paper which includes at least three major sections , developing the following areas: Construct a researched description of several theological and/or philosophical affirmations you hold regarding the human person or human communities. Develop a moral basis for an ethical approach to mental health counseling in relation to your researched description above. Describe an area of advocacy in which you envision practically applying the above ethical approach to a concrete area of justice or service. The paper should include six (6) or more scholarly sources identified through the student ' s own research. (Due April 19) Field Work: Anthropological Observation (10%) A 750- 1000-word observational paper with attendant documentation observing embedded anthropologies in a socio-cultural setting or a person or persons in their relational setting . Choose one. (Due on April 5) Options A or B will be based on " A Phenomenology of Cultural Liturgies " by J.K.A. Smith. Option A: A Christian or another worship observation on the human self before God and with others in religious communities Option B: A common or secular practice , anthropological observation on understandings of the human sel f . Options C or D will be based on the Integration tools from class discussions . Option C: Relational EKG - Interview 2 people to asse s s their positive and negative relations to persons , place s , and activities . Option D: Relational X-Ray – Interview two people on their relational contexts, movements, and progress toward healthy relating. C) Participation A portion of the student's final grade will be based on their participation in class and group discussions, in class exercises, group discussions, and attendance. Full points will be rewarded to those students who attend all class sessions and actively participate in class activities. D) Grading Exams and presentations will be graded the same day. Papers will be graded within 2 weeks. If you have concerns about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator, or program Chair. The students course grade will be based upon her/his mastery of course terms, concept, and objections as demonstrated by classroom attendance, participation, presentations, and papers. Please be on time with all assignments ; if late, there will be a deduction of grade points. Grading is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to monitor their progress. Grading Scale: A 100-94 % C 76-74% A - 93 -90 % C- 73 -70% B+ 89-87% D+ 69-67% B 86-84 % D 66-64% B - 83 -80 % D- 63 -60% C+ 79-77 % F 59% and below E) Course Values Week Item Point Value Week 1: January 6 Class Discussion Board 1 25 Week 2: January 20 Class Discussion Board 2 25 Week 3: February 3 Class Vocational Spiritual Narrative (Due on February 22 ) Discussion Board 3 100 25 Week 4: February 17 Class Article Review 282575 -201905 (Due on March 8 ) Discussion Board 4 200 25 Week 5: March 3 Discussion Board 5 25 Week 6: March 1 7 Discussion Board 6 25 Week 7: March 31 Discussion Board 7 Field Work (Due on April 5) 25 100 Week 8 : April 14 Discussion B oard 8 25 Final Paper Due 288925 -33249 Faith and Counseling Integration Paper (Due April 19 ) 400 Total: 1000 F) Lecture, Reading, and Assignment Outline This guide serves as an outline for the course. Please refer to Discovery for detailed instructions for assignments, reading, and specific due dates for this course. Dates for specific lecture topics may change and material may need to be added or deleted. If any changes are made, they will be announced in class. The Ray S. Anderson Collection
Ray Sherman Anderson (1925–2009) worked the soil and tended the
animals of a South Dakota farm, planted and pastored a church in
Southern California, and completed a PhD degree in theology with
Thomas F. Torrance in New College Edinburgh. He began his pro-
fessional teaching career at Westmont College, and then taught and
served in various administrative capacities at Fuller Theological Semi-
nary for thirty-three years (retiring as Professor Emeritus of Theology
and Ministry). While teaching at Fuller, he served as a parish pastor,
always insisting that theology and ministry go hand-in-hand.
The pastoral theologian who began his teaching career in middle
age penned twenty-seven books. Like Karl Barth, Prof. Anderson ar-
ticulated a theology of and for the church based on God’s own ministry
of revelation and reconciliation in the world. As professor and pas-
tor, he modeled an incarnational, evangelical passion for the healing of
humanity by Jesus Christ, who is both God’s self-revelation to us and
the reconciliation of our broken humanity to the triune God. His gift
of relating suffering and alienated humans to Christ existing as com-
munity (Dietrich Bonhoeffer) is a recurrent motif throughout his life,
ministry, and works.
The Ray S. Anderson Collection comprises books by Ray Ander-
son, an introductory text to his theology by Christian D. Kettler, two
edited volumes that celebrate his distinguished academic career (Incar-
national Ministry: The Presence of Christ in Church, Society, and Family
and On Being Christian . . . and Human), and a reprint of an Edifica-
tion volume that focuses on Ray Anderson’s contributions to the field
of Christian Psychology. A word of gratitude is due to The Society of
Christian Psychology and its parent organization, The American As-
sociation for Christian Counselors, for their permission to make the
Edification issue available in book form. Jim Tedrick of Wipf and Stock
Publishers deserves a special word of thanks for publishing many of
Ray Anderson’s books and commissioning this collection of works to
continue his legacy.
Todd H. Speidell, General Editor

-- 6 of 257 --



-- 7 of 257 --

Introduction
Learning 	comes by 	listening, 	while 	listening 	is a 	skill
acquired through acknowledged ignorance. For twelve years I
have listened to and learned from students enrolled in doctoral
programs in clinical psychology at a theological seminary. As a
theologian with no formal training in psychology, but with
eleven years experience in pastoral counseling, I am keenly
interested in the role of the counselor and/or therapist from the
perspective of Christian theology and ministry.
Unlike many who write in this field, I will not attempt to
integrate of psychology and theology, though I am deeply
interested in such integrative approaches. I write primarily as a
theologian and offer this work as a contribution toward a
theological approach to wholistic therapy. My approach is from
·the perspective of the person who is growing toward a meaning-
ful and effective life with the self, others, and God.
Although my approach leaves me open to criticism from both
psychologists and pastors, I nonetheless advance a basic thesis
in this work. An effective therapeutic intervention is one in
which the client's motive power to grow is facilitated by the
counselor's perceived 	presence 	as a partner 	in the growth
process. This thesis is stated and developed in chapter 3. The
fundamental assumption underlying this thesis is that a theolog-
ical anthropology, or concept of the person, is the key to a
theological basis for integrative work as a Christian counselor
and therapist. My focus is therefore on the role of the person
who counsels, whether it be as a pastor, lay counselor, psycho-
therapist, or family therapist.
Next to the word therapy itself, perhaps the word counseling
is the most ambiguous in this entire discussion. I like the
definition of counseling provided by Roger Hurding in his
marvelous book, The Tree of Healing. Counseling, says Hurd-
ing, is "that activity which aims to help others in any or all
7

-- 8 of 257 --

8 	CHRISTIANS WHO COUNSEL
aspects of their being within a caring relationship." 1 Those who
counsel may be placed on a continuum with some form of
pastoral care on one end 	and the most intensive 	level of
psychotherapy at the other. Christians who counsel will be
found at every point on this continuum. It is for them that this
book is written.
Nothing makes theologians more suspicious than the threat of
"'psychologizing" the gospel. Many talk of the "psychological
captivity" of pastoral care and even theological approaches to
therapy. "'The theologians sat at the feet of the psychiatric
Gamaliels and seemed to like it," writes Paul Pruyser.2 Eugene
Peterson is more dramatic but no less critical when he writes
concerning the effect of psychology on the parish minister:
"'Pastors have been herded into the temple shrines of psychiatry
•.. 	and return to their parishes thoroughly convinced of their
inferiority. "a
As both a theologian and a parish minister, I believe that if
there has been seduction by psychology, it was caused by the
lack of a compelling theology. Note that most of the books
written on the integration of theology and psychology have
come from the psychologists and therapists who sense this need.
Theologians have not been quick to respond, perhaps out of fear
of being enchanted by the wicked witch Psyche. I hope in this
book to calm the fears of pastors and theologians as well as
propose a basis for theological integration to those trained in the
behavioral sciences and therapy.-,
The book is divided into three parts. Part 1 establishes a
theory of counseling based on a model of personhood that is
integrative and wholistic. Part 2 examines the spiritual dynamics
in counseling, beginning with the nature of the kingdom of God
as therapeutic context, followed by chapters on the grace of God,
the Word of God, and prayer. Finally, part 3 explores the nature
of counseling as a calling, a ministry, and a profession and
concludes with two chapters on the moral advocacy of counsel-
ing and the dynamics of pastoral care in counseling praxis.
I offer this book more as a case statement for Christians who
counsel than as a final word on the task of integrating theology
and psychology. In testing these concepts, I have been encour-
aged by a great many students and counselors that this is, at
least, a viable approach. I believe that it is a Christian approach.
And I trust that, in its own way, this book will inspire those who

-- 9 of 257 --

INTRODUCTION 	9
have chosen to exercise their Christians calling by living within
the circle of other people's pain and perplexity. They will find
the Lord already there.
Notes
1. Roger Hurtling, The Tree of Healing: Psychological and Biblical
Foundations for Counseling and Pastoral Care (Grand Rapids: Zonder-
van, 1988), 26.
2. Paul 	Pruyser, 	The Minister as Diagnostician (Philadelphia:
Wesbninster, 1976), 23.
3. Eugene Peterson, Five Smooth Stones for Pastoral Work (Atlanta:
John Knox, 1980), 112-13.
4. From a growing list of books on integration, I mention just a few:
David G. Benner, ed., Christian Counseling and Psychotherapy
(Grand Rapids: Baker, 1987); Psychotherapy in Christian Perspective
(Grand Rapids: Baker, 1987); and Psychotherapy and the Spiritual
Quest (Grand Rapids: Baker, 1988).
John D. Carter and Bruce Narramore, The Integration of Psychology
and Theology: An Introduction (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1979).
Gary R. Collins, The Rebuilding of Psychology: An Integration of
Psychology and Christianity (Wheaton, Ill.: Tyndale House, 1977);
Christian Counseling-A 	Comprehensive Guide (Dallas: Word Books,
1988); and Psychology and Theology: Prospects for Integration (Nash-
ville: Abingdon, 1981).
C. Stephen Evans, Wisdom and Humanness in Psychology-Pros-
pects for a Christian Approach (Grand Rapids: Baker, 1989).
Kirk Farnsworth, Wholehearted Integration: Harmonizing Psychol-
ogy and Christianity Through Word and Deed (Grand Rapids: Baker,
1985).
J. R. Fleck and John D. Carter, eds., Psychology and Christianity:
Integration Readings (Nashville: Abingdon, 1981).
Stanton L. Jones, editor, Psychology and the Christian Faith-An
Introductory Reader (Grand Rapids: Baker, 1986).
Ronald 	L. Koteskey, Psychology from 	a Christian Perspective
(Nashville: Abingdon, 1980).
R. E. Larzelere, "The Task Ahead: Six Levels of Integration 	of
Christianity and Psychology," Journal of Psychology and Theology 8
(1980): 3-11.

to Controversial Issues (Lanham, Md.: University Press of America,
